## Introduction

Few moral questions cut as deeply into the soul of a nation as the question of abortion. In Nigeria, where faith, family, and the sanctity of human life form the bedrock of our shared identity, this question admits of only one honest answer: the unborn child is a human being, and the deliberate termination of that life cannot be reconciled with justice, morality, or the values that define us as a people. Nigeria's existing laws, which criminalize abortion except where the mother's life is endangered, are not relics of a bygone era to be discarded at the prompting of foreign lobbyists. They are the considered expression of a civilization that refuses to accept the killing of the innocent as a solution to human difficulty. These laws protect not only individual lives but the very future of our nation and the wellbeing of our women. They must be preserved, defended, and enforced.

## The Unborn Child Is a Human Being

The foundational argument for criminalization is biological, not merely religious. From the moment of conception, a genetically distinct, living human organism comes into existence. This is not theology; it is embryology. The zygote possesses a complete human genome, initiates its own development, and, if not interfered with, will grow into the same kind of being as the reader of this essay. To deny the humanity of the unborn is to engage in arbitrary line-drawing—at viability, at the first breath, at some chosen trimester—none of which can bear the moral weight placed upon it. If the unborn child is human, and if human beings possess an inherent right to life, then abortion is the taking of an innocent human life. No appeal to convenience, privacy, or autonomy can overcome this basic truth.

## The Weight of Nigerian Law and Constitutional Values

Sections 228, 229, and 230 of the Criminal Code, along with Sections 232 through 236 of the Penal Code, criminalize abortion except to save the mother's life. These provisions are not accidents of colonial inheritance; they reflect a coherent legal philosophy embraced and retained by successive Nigerian parliaments. Section 33(1) of the 1999 Constitution guarantees every person a right to life, and a principled reading of that right—one that does not arbitrarily exclude the most vulnerable human beings—must extend its protection to the unborn. To legalize abortion would be to carve out a class of human beings whom the state deems killable at the discretion of others. Such a precedent is constitutionally and morally intolerable.

## The Voice of Religion and Culture

Nigeria is a deeply religious society. Christianity teaches that human beings are made in the image of God and that life is sacred from its earliest stages—"Before I formed you in the womb I knew you" (Jeremiah 1:5). Islam likewise affirms the sanctity of unborn life, with the overwhelming consensus of scholars prohibiting abortion save in narrowly defined circumstances. Traditional African worldviews, from Yoruba to Igbo to Hausa-Fulani, honor the child in the womb as a gift, a continuation of ancestry, and a bond between generations. Children are not burdens in our cultural imagination; they are blessings, proverbs made flesh, the living promise that a people will continue. Any policy that legitimizes abortion stands in direct opposition to the convictions of the vast majority of Nigerians. In a democracy, law must reflect the moral consensus of the people it governs—not be imposed upon them by external actors funded from abroad.

## The Moral Continuity of Innocent Life: What the Womb Does Not Change

At the heart of this debate lies a question that abortion advocates prefer to leave unasked: what exactly is the moral difference between a child one day before birth and a child one day after? What changes, in the minute between the final contraction and the first cry, that transforms a being the law may destroy into a being the law must protect? The honest answer is: nothing of moral significance. Location changes. The child moves from inside the womb to outside it. But location has never before been thought to confer or remove humanity. A man in his house and a man on the street are equally human; a citizen in Lagos and a citizen in Kano are equally Nigerian; a child in the womb and a child in the cradle are equally persons.

Nigerian law already recognizes this truth wherever it is not clouded by the politics of abortion. Our Criminal Code punishes murder with the gravest penalty known to our legal system. We do not ask whether the victim was old or young, famous or obscure, wanted or unwanted. The taking of innocent human life is murder, and murder is condemned without qualification. This is the moral architecture of a civilized society, and it is the architecture on which Nigerian law rightly stands.

Our history contains a sobering witness to this principle. In parts of what is now Cross River and Akwa Ibom, the practice of killing twin infants was once widespread, rooted in the belief that their birth was a curse. The cause of ending this practice is associated above all with Mary Slessor, the Scottish missionary who, together with Efik reformers and converts, labored to rescue abandoned twins and to transform the moral conscience of the communities she served. We now look back on the killing of twins with unambiguous moral clarity: it was barbaric, it was wrong, and its abolition is remembered as one of the noblest chapters in our region's history. No serious Nigerian would today suggest that the twin infant was less than fully human because her community considered her inconvenient or unwanted.

But if we condemn the killing of the twin infant on her first day of life, on what coherent ground do we permit the killing of any child on her last day in the womb? On her last month? On her last trimester? At any point in her gestation? The child in the womb possesses the same human nature, the same genetic identity, the same trajectory of development as the child in the cradle. She is simply younger and smaller and more hidden. If vulnerability, smallness, and hiddenness were grounds for the withdrawal of legal protection, the whole edifice of human rights would collapse overnight. The principle of our law is precisely the opposite: the more vulnerable the human being, the greater the claim on our protection.

This is why the moral logic of abortion cannot be contained. If it is permissible to end the life of a child in the eighth month of pregnancy because she is inconvenient to her mother, it is permissible—by the same logic—to end her life in the first hour after birth for the same reason. The location of the child has not changed her nature; only our willingness to see her has changed. Conversely, if we recognize that the killing of the newborn is murder, and that the historical killing of twins was a grave injustice abolished with honor, then we must recognize that the killing of the unborn stands in the same moral category. There is no line in the uterus behind which humanity begins. There is only one human life, developing from conception onward, deserving of protection at every stage.

To criminalize abortion is therefore not to impose a peculiar or sectarian rule on Nigerian society. It is simply to apply the same principle we already apply everywhere else: that innocent human life may not be deliberately destroyed. The burden of argument lies not on those who defend this consistency but on those who would make an arbitrary exception to it—an exception that costs hundreds of thousands of lives and that no serious moral reasoning can sustain.

## Nigeria's Demographic Future: Every Life Is a National Asset

Beyond the moral and spiritual case, there is an argument that speaks directly to Nigeria's destiny as a nation. By 2050, Nigeria is projected to become the third most populous country in the world, surpassed only by India and China. This is not a liability—it is the single greatest strategic advantage our nation possesses in the century ahead. While much of the world, from Europe to East Asia, faces catastrophic population decline, aging workforces, and collapsing pension systems, Nigeria stands on the threshold of a demographic dividend unmatched in human history.

Every unborn child is a future citizen. Every unborn child is a potential farmer who will feed our cities, a potential engineer who will build our bridges, a potential doctor who will heal our sick, a potential teacher who will shape the next generation, a potential entrepreneur who will create the industries of tomorrow. The child aborted today is the innovator, soldier, poet, or president we will not have in 2050. A nation that tolerates the systematic destruction of its future generations is a nation voluntarily surrendering its place on the world stage.

The lessons of other nations are stark. Japan, South Korea, and much of Europe now face demographic winters so severe that their governments offer cash incentives for childbirth, import foreign labor at great social cost, and still cannot reverse the decline. These societies largely embraced permissive abortion regimes decades ago; they are now living with the consequences. Nigeria must not repeat their mistake. To legalize abortion at the precise moment when our youthful population is poised to become the engine of African and global growth would be an act of self-sabotage without historical parallel.

Critics will object that population growth strains resources. But people are not merely mouths to feed—they are minds to think, hands to build, and hearts to serve. Every great economic transformation in modern history, from the Industrial Revolution to the rise of the Asian Tigers, has been powered by human beings. Nigeria's path to prosperity runs through its people, not around them. Our task is not to reduce our population but to educate, employ, and empower it. Criminalizing abortion preserves the human capital on which that future depends.

## The Hidden Wounds: Abortion and the Mental Health of Women

Among the most forcefully advanced claims of abortion advocates is that legalization serves the wellbeing of women. But the evidence tells a different story—one that proponents rarely confront honestly. A growing body of peer-reviewed research documents that abortion leaves lasting psychological scars on a significant portion of women who undergo it. Priscilla Coleman's 2011 meta-analysis, published in the *British Journal of Psychiatry*, drew on twenty-two studies involving over 877,000 women and found that those who had abortions experienced an 81 percent increased risk of mental health problems compared to women who had not, including markedly elevated rates of depression, anxiety, substance abuse, and suicidal ideation. Other researchers have described the cluster of symptoms observed in post-abortive women as Post-Abortion Syndrome—a recognizable pattern of grief, guilt, intrusive memories, emotional numbness, and self-destructive behavior.

These findings accord with what pastors, imams, counselors, and family members have long observed: that the termination of a pregnancy is rarely the clean, consequence-free event advocates portray. It is a rupture—between mother and child, between a woman and her own conscience, between the self she was and the self she becomes. Many women carry this wound in silence for years, sometimes for life.

Now consider the Nigerian context. Our mental health infrastructure is, by any honest accounting, severely underdeveloped. Nigeria has fewer than 300 practicing psychiatrists for a population exceeding 200 million. Access to trained therapists and counselors is almost nonexistent outside major urban centers. Cultural stigma around mental illness remains profound, and women who suffer silently are often told to pray harder, endure quietly, or simply "move on." Into this context, abortion advocates propose to import a practice that produces, in the most careful studies, substantial psychological harm—without any corresponding system to diagnose, treat, or accompany the women left broken in its wake.

This is not compassion. It is abandonment dressed in the language of choice. To hand a Nigerian woman a termination and then release her back into a society with no capacity to heal the trauma that follows is to inflict a double injury: first the loss of her child, then the solitary burden of grief she cannot name and cannot treat. The advocates who champion legalization will not be there at 3:00 a.m. when she cannot sleep; they will not sit with her when the anniversaries come; they will not bear the weight of her silent sorrow.

True compassion for women recognizes that the woman in crisis and the child in her womb are not adversaries but companions, both deserving of protection. It refuses to solve one person's difficulty by destroying another and wounding the first in the process. It invests, instead, in the systems that help women flourish: prenatal care, maternal support, economic opportunity, community solidarity, and yes, mental health services for those already carrying the grief of past abortions. A pro-woman society does not offer its daughters a harm and call it freedom. It offers them protection, accompaniment, and the conditions for genuine flourishing.

## Refuting the "Safe Abortion" Argument

Proponents of legalization argue that criminalization merely drives abortion underground, producing unsafe procedures and maternal deaths. This argument, though presented with compassion, is ultimately flawed. First, it concedes that abortion is dangerous—and then proposes that the solution is to make it more common. Second, the logic would justify legalizing any harmful act on the grounds that it occurs clandestinely. Third, even the "safest" legal abortion carries the psychological harms documented above; physical safety does not equate to wholeness. Fourth, the true response to unsafe abortions is not to sanction the killing of the unborn but to attack the underlying causes: poverty, inadequate maternal healthcare, the abandonment of women by irresponsible men, and the lack of support structures for pregnant mothers. Nigeria must invest in prenatal care, maternal welfare, adoption systems, and family support—real solutions that honor both mother and child, rather than false solutions that sacrifice one to wound the other.

## Protecting Women, Not Abandoning Them

A genuinely pro-woman society does not offer women the scalpel as its answer to crisis. It offers them community, medical care, economic support, and dignity. Legalized abortion too often becomes a tool by which men escape responsibility, families escape obligation, and society escapes the duty to care for its mothers and children. To criminalize abortion is not to turn our backs on women in difficult circumstances; it is to commit ourselves to building the kinds of institutions—maternity homes, adoption networks, social welfare programs, church and mosque-based support, and trauma-informed counseling services—that enable women to choose life without fear and to heal when wounded. The measure of a nation is how it treats its weakest members, and the unborn child, together with the vulnerable mother, stands at the center of that test.

## The Slippery Slope of Legalization

Wherever abortion has been legalized under the banner of "limited exceptions," those limits have eroded with time. What begins as permission in cases of rape or fetal abnormality expands into abortion on demand, often at advanced stages of pregnancy. The cumulative toll on these societies—measured in tens of millions of unborn lives lost, demographic collapse, and a quiet epidemic of wounded women—is a warning Nigeria cannot afford to ignore. Once the principle that the unborn may be killed is admitted into law, there is no stable place to draw the line. The only coherent and defensible stance is to maintain the current prohibition, with the narrow exception for saving the mother's life, and to resist all pressure—domestic or foreign—to weaken it.

## Conclusion

The criminalization of abortion in Nigeria is not a failure of modernity; it is a triumph of moral clarity, national foresight, and genuine concern for the wellbeing of women. It affirms that every human life, however small and however inconvenient, belongs to the human family and deserves the protection of law. It treats the child in the womb with the same seriousness we rightly accord the newborn, the twin infant once saved from abandonment, and every other innocent life our Criminal Code shields from destruction. It aligns our statutes with our Constitution, our faith traditions, our cultural heritage, and the biological reality of human development. It safeguards the demographic strength on which Nigeria's future as a great nation depends. And it shields our daughters, sisters, and mothers from a practice that, however smoothly marketed, leaves behind a trail of hidden suffering our society is ill-equipped to heal. The path forward for Nigeria is not to imitate the moral confusions of other societies, but to stand firmly on the side of life—protecting the unborn child, supporting the struggling mother, building a nation populated and powered by its own people. On this question, Nigeria must not waver. Life must be defended, and the law must continue to defend it.

If the unborn child is human, and if human beings possess an inherent right to life, then abortion is the taking of an innocent human life. Section 33(1) of the 1999 Constitution guarantees every person a right to life—and a principled reading of that right, one that does not arbitrarily exclude the most vulnerable human beings among us, must extend its protection to the child in the womb. To legalize abortion would be to carve out a class of human beings whom the state declares killable at another's discretion. No constitutional order worthy of the name can tolerate such a carve-out.

## The Voice of Religion and Culture

What the law protects, the conscience of the Nigerian people already honors. Christianity teaches that human beings are made in the image of God and known by their Creator before they are formed in the womb: "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you" (Jeremiah 1:5). To legalize abortion would be to set our laws against the deepest convictions of our people, at the instruction of foreign voices funded from abroad. In a democracy worthy of the name, law must express the moral consensus of the governed—not override it.

## The Moral Continuity of Innocent Life: What the Womb Does Not Change

At the heart of the abortion debate lies a question its defenders prefer never to be asked: what is the moral difference between a child one day before birth and one day after? What changes, in the minute between the final contraction and the first cry, that transforms a being the law may destroy into a being the law must protect? The honest answer is: nothing of moral significance. Location changes. The child passes from inside the womb to outside it. But location has never before conferred or removed humanity. Every unborn child is a future citizen. Every great economic transformation—from the Industrial Revolution to the rise of the Asian Tigers—was powered by human beings. Nigeria's path to prosperity runs through its people, not around them. Our task is not to reduce our population but to educate, employ, and empower it. Criminalizing abortion preserves the human capital on which our future depends.

## The Hidden Wounds: Abortion and the Mental Health of Women

The loudest claim of abortion's defenders is that legalization serves women. The evidence tells another story. A substantial body of peer-reviewed research documents that abortion leaves lasting psychological scars on a significant portion of women who undergo it. Priscilla Coleman's 2011 meta-analysis in the *British Journal of Psychiatry*, drawing on twenty-two studies and more than 877,000 women, found that those who aborted experienced an 81 percent increased risk of mental health problems—depression, anxiety, substance abuse, suicidal ideation—compared to those who did not. Clinicians have given the cluster of symptoms a name: Post-Abortion Syndrome. Grief. Guilt. Intrusive memories. Emotional numbness. Self-destruction.

These findings match what pastors, imams, mothers, and counselors have long known in their own communities: that the ending of a pregnancy is rarely the clean, consequence-free act its defenders describe. It is a rupture—between mother and child, between a woman and her own conscience, between the self she was and the self she becomes. Many women carry that wound in silence for years, sometimes for life.

Now place that reality within the Nigerian context. We have fewer than 300 practicing psychiatrists for a population of over 200 million. Trained counselors are almost unknown outside the major cities. The stigma around mental illness remains crushing; suffering women are told to pray harder, endure quietly, move on. Into this landscape, abortion's advocates propose to introduce a practice the careful studies show produces substantial psychological harm—without any system to receive, diagnose, or heal the women it will wound.

This is not compassion. It is abandonment dressed in the vocabulary of choice. To offer a Nigerian woman a termination and then return her to a society that cannot tend the trauma that follows is to inflict a double injury: first the loss of her child, then a solitary grief she cannot name and cannot treat. The activists who preach legalization will not be at her bedside at three in the morning. They will not sit with her on the anniversaries. They will not carry the weight of her silent sorrow.

True compassion refuses to pit woman against child. It recognizes that the mother in crisis and the life within her are not adversaries but companions, both deserving of protection. It does not solve one person's difficulty by destroying another and breaking the first in the process. It invests in what actually helps women flourish: prenatal care, maternal support, economic opportunity, community solidarity, trauma-informed counseling for those already grieving. A pro-woman society does not hand its daughters a harm and call it freedom. It gives them protection, accompaniment, and the conditions for genuine flourishing.

## Refuting the "Safe Abortion" Argument

Abortion's advocates fall back on a final claim: that criminalization only drives the practice underground and kills women in unsafe procedures. The argument collapses under scrutiny. It concedes that abortion is dangerous—and then demands we make it more common. By the same logic, every harmful act would be legalized on the grounds that it occurs clandestinely. Even the safest legal abortion carries the psychological wounds already documented; physical safety is not wholeness. The answer is to build up prenatal care, maternal welfare, adoption systems, and family support. That is how a nation honors both mother and child—not by sacrificing one to wound the other.

## The Slippery Slope of Legalization

Every society that has legalized abortion under the banner of "limited exceptions" has watched those limits crumble. Permission for rape and fetal abnormality becomes abortion on demand, in some jurisdictions up to the moment of birth. The cumulative cost—tens of millions of unborn lives lost, demographic collapse, a quiet epidemic of wounded women—stands as a warning Nigeria cannot afford to ignore. Once the principle that the unborn may be killed enters the law, no stable line remains. The only coherent position is the one we already hold: prohibition, with the narrow exception for saving the mother's life, maintained against every pressure foreign or domestic to weaken it.

## Conclusion: A Call to the Conscience of the Nation

Nigeria stands at a crossroads that few nations ever face with such clarity. Before us lies the path of life: the path our Constitution marks, our Criminal Code enforces, our Bible and our Qur'an bless, our ancestors paved, our future demands, and our women deserve. Behind the other path lies the dust of nations that chose otherwise—nations that traded their unborn children for a promise of liberation and received, in return, empty cradles, aging cities, wounded women, and a silence where the laughter of the next generation should have been.

Let us not follow them. Let us instead be the nation that remembers. Remembers that the child in the womb is the same child who will be placed in her mother's arms. Remembers that the infant once thrown away as a cursed twin was a person, and that Mary Slessor and those who stood with her were right. Remembers that our laws against murder protect the weak precisely because they are weak, and that no human being is weaker or more innocent than a child before birth. Remembers that our greatest national resource is not our oil beneath the Niger Delta but our children yet to be born, in whose minds and hands the Nigeria of 2050 will rise or fall. Remembers that a woman in crisis deserves more than a scalpel—she deserves a community, a family, a nation that stands with her and her child alike.

To the legislator tempted by foreign money and foreign arguments: do not betray the people who sent you to Abuja. To the judge weighing the meaning of Section 33: read it as the framers meant it—every person, including the smallest. To the pastor, the imam, the elder, the teacher, the parent: speak. The voices urging Nigeria to abandon her unborn children are loud and well-funded, but they are not right, and they are not ours. To every Nigerian of conscience: this is a moment to be counted.

The unborn child cannot speak for herself. She cannot march, petition, vote, or plead. She has only the voice we give her, and the law we make for her sake. Let that voice be unmistakable, and let that law be unshaken. Let Nigeria be remembered, among all the nations of the twenty-first century, as the great country that chose life—fully, courageously, without apology, and without exception. On this question, we must not waver. Life must be defended. And the law must continue to defend it.

# In Defense of the Unborn: Why Nigeria Must Uphold the Criminalization of Abortion

## Introduction

Few moral questions cut as deeply as the question of abortion, and few nations have answered it as clearly as Nigeria. From the sacred texts that shape our faith, to the customary traditions that bind our communities, to the statutes inscribed in our criminal law, Nigeria has long affirmed a single truth: the life of the unborn child is precious and worthy of protection. This essay argues, without apology, that abortion should remain criminalized in Nigeria, and that any attempt to liberalize our laws would be a betrayal of our legal heritage, our religious convictions, our cultural identity, and our moral duty to the most defenseless members of the human family.

## I. Nigerian Law Has Always Protected the Unborn

Nigeria's legal framework speaks with unmistakable clarity on this issue. Under **Sections 228, 229, and 230 of the Criminal Code Act**, which applies across the southern states, the law criminalizes procuring a miscarriage. Section 228 punishes any person who, with intent to procure a miscarriage, administers poison, uses force, or employs any means whatsoever, with imprisonment for up to fourteen years. Section 229 extends this liability to the pregnant woman herself who attempts to procure her own miscarriage, prescribing a term of up to seven years. Section 230 penalizes those who supply drugs or instruments intended for such purposes.

In the northern states, **Sections 232, 233, 234, and 235 of the Penal Code** reinforce the same principle. Section 232 criminalizes causing a miscarriage, while Section 233 imposes even harsher penalties where the woman is "quick with child." Section 235 punishes those who cause the death of a quick unborn child by acts aimed at the mother. Section 236 addresses death caused to an unborn child by acts intended to prevent its being born alive.

Together, these provisions form a consistent legal architecture: the Nigerian state, through both colonial-inherited and post-independence enactments, has treated the unborn as a life deserving of criminal-law protection. Only where the life of the mother is in genuine jeopardy does the law recognize a narrow exception. This is not an accident of legislation — it is a deliberate moral choice, reaffirmed by successive generations of lawmakers. To decriminalize abortion would be to tear out a thread woven deeply into the fabric of Nigerian jurisprudence.

## II. Our Religious Heritage Affirms the Sanctity of Life

Nigeria is one of the most devout nations on earth. Both Christianity and Islam — the faiths of the overwhelming majority of our people — teach unambiguously that human life begins at conception and that the deliberate destruction of an unborn child is a grave moral wrong.

The Christian tradition draws from Scripture passages such as Psalm 139:13 — "For You formed my inward parts; You wove me in my mother's womb" — and Jeremiah 1:5 — "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you." These verses present the unborn not as clusters of tissue but as persons known and loved by God before birth. The Catholic Church, the Anglican Communion, and virtually every major Nigerian Christian denomination teach that abortion is a violation of the Fifth Commandment.

Islamic teaching, grounded in the Qur'an and Hadith, likewise condemns the killing of the unborn. Qur'an 17:31 warns: "Do not kill your children for fear of poverty. We shall provide for them and for you. Killing them is a great sin." Classical Islamic jurisprudence, across the Maliki school predominant in northern Nigeria, strongly restricts abortion, particularly after ensoulment, and treats it as a violation of a life under divine protection.

When two faiths that shape the moral compass of more than 95% of Nigerians speak with one voice, the state has every reason — and every right — to reflect that moral consensus in its laws.

## III. African Cultural Values Honor the Child in the Womb

Long before colonial statutes, African tradition treated pregnancy as sacred. Among the Igbo, the Yoruba, the Hausa-Fulani, the Tiv, the Ijaw, and countless other peoples, an unborn child is regarded as a gift from God and the ancestors — a future member of the community whose arrival is anticipated with joy, ritual, and prayer. Naming ceremonies, prenatal blessings, and communal protection of pregnant women all testify to a worldview in which the child in the womb is already *someone*, already a relation, already belonging.

To speak of "discarding" such a life is alien to our cultural imagination. Traditional African ethics — often summed up in the principle of *ubuntu*, "I am because we are" — insists that every human being is constituted by relationships, including the relationship between mother and unborn child. To legalize abortion is to import a foreign individualism that treats the womb as private property rather than a sacred trust. Nigeria must not allow external pressures, no matter how well-funded or well-branded, to erode a cultural inheritance that has sustained our peoples for millennia.

## IV. The Moral Duty to Protect the Defenseless

At the heart of any just society lies a simple principle: the strong must not be permitted to destroy the weak. The unborn child is the weakest among us — voiceless, dependent, utterly unable to defend itself. If the law exists to protect anyone, it exists to protect those who cannot protect themselves.

Scientifically, there is no serious dispute that human life begins at conception. From fertilization, a genetically distinct, living human organism exists, developing continuously through the stages we all once passed through. The moral line between "person" and "non-person" drawn at some arbitrary later point — viability, a trimester, birth itself — is philosophically incoherent. Either every human life has intrinsic dignity, or none of us can be sure that ours does.

Arguments from "hard cases" — poverty, unwanted pregnancy, even assault — are genuinely painful, and society owes such women compassion, medical care, economic support, and justice against their attackers. But compassion does not require killing the innocent. The answer to a woman in crisis is to lift her up, not to end the life of her child. A civilization is measured not by how it treats the powerful but by how it shelters the powerless.

## V. Conclusion

Nigeria stands at a crossroads where foreign lobbying, international donor pressure, and shifting global fashions urge us to abandon our protections for the unborn. We must refuse. Our Criminal Code and Penal Code did not criminalize abortion by accident; our churches and mosques did not condemn it by whim; our ancestors did not cherish the womb by superstition. These convergent witnesses — legal, religious, cultural, and moral — point to one conclusion: abortion is the taking of an innocent human life, and a just Nigeria will continue to say so.

To uphold the criminalization of abortion is not cruelty; it is fidelity — fidelity to our laws, to our faith, to our heritage, and to the voiceless child whose only hope of protection lies in the conscience of a nation willing to stand for life.

# In Defense of the Unborn: Why Nigeria Must Uphold the Criminalization of Abortion

## Introduction

Few moral questions cut as deeply as the question of abortion, and few nations have answered it as clearly as Nigeria. From the sacred texts that shape our faith, to the customary traditions that bind our communities, to the statutes inscribed in our criminal law, Nigeria has long affirmed a single truth: the life of the unborn child is precious and worthy of protection. This essay argues, without apology, that abortion should remain criminalized in Nigeria, and that any attempt to liberalize our laws would be a betrayal of our legal heritage, our religious convictions, our cultural identity, our demographic destiny, and our moral duty to the most defenseless members of the human family.

## I. Nigerian Law Has Always Protected the Unborn

Nigeria's legal framework speaks with unmistakable clarity on this issue. Under **Sections 228, 229, and 230 of the Criminal Code Act**, which applies across the southern states, the law criminalizes procuring a miscarriage. Section 228 punishes any person who, with intent to procure a miscarriage, administers poison, uses force, or employs any means whatsoever, with imprisonment for up to fourteen years. Section 229 extends this liability to the pregnant woman herself who attempts to procure her own miscarriage, prescribing a term of up to seven years. Section 230 penalizes those who supply drugs or instruments intended for such purposes.

In the northern states, **Sections 232, 233, 234, and 235 of the Penal Code** reinforce the same principle. Section 232 criminalizes causing a miscarriage, while Section 233 imposes even harsher penalties where the woman is "quick with child." Section 235 punishes those who cause the death of a quick unborn child by acts aimed at the mother. Section 236 addresses death caused to an unborn child by acts intended to prevent its being born alive.

Together, these provisions form a consistent legal architecture: the Nigerian state, through both colonial-inherited and post-independence enactments, has treated the unborn as a life deserving of criminal-law protection. Only where the life of the mother is in genuine jeopardy does the law recognize a narrow exception. This is not an accident of legislation — it is a deliberate moral choice, reaffirmed by successive generations of lawmakers. To decriminalize abortion would be to tear out a thread woven deeply into the fabric of Nigerian jurisprudence.

## II. Our Religious Heritage Affirms the Sanctity of Life

Nigeria is one of the most devout nations on earth. Both Christianity and Islam — the faiths of the overwhelming majority of our people — teach unambiguously that human life begins at conception and that the deliberate destruction of an unborn child is a grave moral wrong.

The Christian tradition draws from Scripture passages such as Psalm 139:13 — "For You formed my inward parts; You wove me in my mother's womb" — and Jeremiah 1:5 — "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you." These verses present the unborn not as clusters of tissue but as persons known and loved by God before birth. The Catholic Church, the Anglican Communion, and virtually every major Nigerian Christian denomination teach that abortion is a violation of the Fifth Commandment.

Islamic teaching, grounded in the Qur'an and Hadith, likewise condemns the killing of the unborn. Qur'an 17:31 warns: "Do not kill your children for fear of poverty. We shall provide for them and for you. Killing them is a great sin." Classical Islamic jurisprudence, across the Maliki school predominant in northern Nigeria, strongly restricts abortion, particularly after ensoulment, and treats it as a violation of a life under divine protection.

When two faiths that shape the moral compass of more than 95% of Nigerians speak with one voice, the state has every reason — and every right — to reflect that moral consensus in its laws.

## III. African Cultural Values Honor the Child in the Womb

Long before colonial statutes, African tradition treated pregnancy as sacred. Among the Igbo, the Yoruba, the Hausa-Fulani, the Tiv, the Ijaw, and countless other peoples, an unborn child is regarded as a gift from God and the ancestors — a future member of the community whose arrival is anticipated with joy, ritual, and prayer. Naming ceremonies, prenatal blessings, and communal protection of pregnant women all testify to a worldview in which the child in the womb is already *someone*, already a relation, already belonging.

To speak of "discarding" such a life is alien to our cultural imagination. Traditional African ethics — often summed up in the principle of *ubuntu*, "I am because we are" — insists that every human being is constituted by relationships, including the relationship between mother and unborn child. To legalize abortion is to import a foreign individualism that treats the womb as private property rather than a sacred trust. Nigeria must not allow external pressures, no matter how well-funded or well-branded, to erode a cultural inheritance that has sustained our peoples for millennia.

## IV. The Unborn and the Future of the Nigerian Nation

Beyond the moral and spiritual dimensions lies a stark demographic and developmental truth: **every abortion is the loss of a future Nigerian**. By 2050, the United Nations projects that Nigeria will become the third most populous nation on earth, surpassing the United States. While some voices frame this as a crisis, visionary leadership recognizes it for what it truly is: an extraordinary opportunity. Our youth are, and will remain, our single greatest national resource.

Consider what every terminated pregnancy actually represents. It is not an abstraction. It is a future doctor who will never treat patients in Enugu. It is a future agricultural scientist who will never help feed the Sahel. It is a future engineer who will never design the bridges of Lagos, a future teacher who will never stand before a classroom in Kano, a future entrepreneur who will never build the company that employs thousands. It is a future poet, a future judge, a future mother, a future president. Each of these lives carries within it an entire world of possibility — possibility that abortion extinguishes forever.

Nations that have embraced abortion on demand are today confronting the consequences of their choices: collapsing birthrates, aging populations, shrinking workforces, and economies straining under the weight of demographic decline. Japan, much of Europe, and parts of East Asia now spend enormous sums trying to reverse the very trend they once celebrated. Nigeria need not repeat their mistake. Our youthful population is not a burden to be managed through population-control programs; it is the engine of our coming prosperity — provided we have the wisdom to let it live.

To permit widespread abortion is to inflict a self-inflicted wound on the Nigerian future. It is to trade generations of talent for the fleeting convenience of the present. A nation serious about development invests in its children, born and unborn, through maternal healthcare, economic support for families, education, and opportunity — not through the elimination of the next generation before it draws breath.

## V. Nigerian Sovereignty and the Foreign Agenda

It must be said plainly: the push to liberalize Nigeria's abortion laws is not, in the main, rising from the Nigerian grassroots. Poll after poll confirms that Nigerians — across regions, religions, and ethnic groups — overwhelmingly oppose abortion on demand. So where is the pressure coming from? Increasingly, it comes from well-funded international organizations such as the **International Planned Parenthood Federation (IPPF)**, **MSI Reproductive Choices (formerly Marie Stopes International)**, and a constellation of Western donor agencies and foundations that channel vast sums into "reproductive rights" advocacy across Africa.

These organizations operate with budgets that dwarf those of local Nigerian civil society groups. They fund workshops, train journalists, draft model legislation, sponsor "sensitization" campaigns, and cultivate relationships with lawmakers and ministries. Their framing — the language of "safe abortion," "reproductive justice," "unmet need" — is carefully crafted to make the destruction of unborn life sound like healthcare and to make Nigeria's longstanding moral consensus sound like backwardness.

This is not partnership; it is a soft form of ideological colonialism. The same Western capitals that once imposed their commerce and their borders upon Africa now seek to impose their sexual and demographic orthodoxies. It is telling that while these organizations lecture African nations on "choice," their home governments simultaneously worry about their own falling birthrates. The question Nigerians must ask is simple: *Why are the people most concerned about African population growth not Africans themselves?*

Nigerian sovereignty demands that our laws reflect Nigerian values, debated by Nigerian citizens, enacted by Nigerian legislators, and rooted in Nigerian moral tradition — not conditions attached to foreign aid, not templates written in London or New York, and not the policy preferences of organizations whose ideological commitments predate and override any genuine concern for African flourishing. To yield on abortion is to concede that our moral vision is negotiable in exchange for foreign approval. It is not.

## VI. The Moral Duty to Protect the Defenseless

At the heart of any just society lies a simple principle: the strong must not be permitted to destroy the weak. But compassion does not require killing the innocent. The answer to a woman in crisis is to lift her up, not to end the life of her child. A civilization is measured not by how it treats the powerful but by how it shelters the powerless.

## VII. Conclusion

Nigeria stands at a crossroads. Foreign lobbying, international donor pressure, and shifting global fashions urge us to abandon our protections for the unborn. We must refuse. Our Criminal Code and Penal Code did not criminalize abortion by accident; our churches and mosques did not condemn it by whim; our ancestors did not cherish the womb by superstition; our demographic destiny does not call for self-destruction; and our sovereignty is not a commodity to be traded for the approval of outsiders. These convergent witnesses — legal, religious, cultural, demographic, national, and moral — point to one conclusion: abortion is the taking of an innocent human life, and a just Nigeria will continue to say so.

To uphold the criminalization of abortion is not cruelty; it is fidelity — fidelity to our laws, to our faith, to our heritage, to our future, and to the voiceless child whose only hope of protection lies in the conscience of a nation willing to stand for life.

To yield on abortion is to concede that our moral vision is negotiable in exchange for foreign approval. It is not.

## VI. The Maternal Mortality Myth: Confronting the Opposition's Trump Card

No argument is deployed more often or more emotionally in favor of liberalization than the claim that Nigeria must legalize abortion to save women's lives. The figure most commonly cited — roughly 6,000 maternal deaths per year attributed to "unsafe abortion," drawn from Guttmacher Institute estimates — is presented as though it settles the debate. **First, the real drivers of maternal mortality in Nigeria are not restrictive abortion laws.** According to the World Health Organization and Nigeria's own Federal Ministry of Health, the leading causes of maternal death in this country are postpartum hemorrhage, eclampsia and pre-eclampsia, obstructed labor, puerperal sepsis, and complications of unsafe delivery — driven by the chronic shortage of skilled birth attendants, the scarcity of functional primary health centers, inadequate emergency obstetric care, poor rural transport, and persistent poverty. These are the true killers of Nigerian mothers. To pretend that changing the abortion statute would meaningfully address them is to misdiagnose the disease and prescribe the wrong medicine. The scalpel to Nigerian maternal mortality is investment in hospitals, midwives, ambulances, blood banks, and antenatal care — not the legalization of killing the unborn.

**Second, the "unsafe abortion" figures themselves must be scrutinized with care.** Many of the most widely circulated numbers originate from advocacy organizations with an explicit policy agenda. They rely on modeling assumptions, extrapolations, and self-serving definitions in which any complication following an abortion attempt is counted as proof that restriction itself is the cause. This is circular reasoning: having defined the problem as "unsafe abortion," the only solution considered is legalization. But the alternative explanation — that these tragedies stem from poverty, clandestine practitioners, and health-system failure, all of which exist regardless of the legal regime — is systematically ignored.

**Third, the example of Chile shatters the legalization narrative completely.** For decades, Chile maintained one of the strictest abortion prohibitions in the world. Yet during that same period, Chile achieved one of the *lowest* maternal mortality rates in all of Latin America — lower than many countries with permissive abortion laws. How? Through sustained investment in maternal healthcare: trained birth attendants, accessible prenatal care, emergency obstetric services, and education for mothers. Chilean researchers including Dr. Elard Koch have documented that maternal mortality in Chile fell dramatically over decades *while* abortion remained illegal, demonstrating beyond reasonable dispute that the decisive variable is healthcare quality, not abortion access. Poland tells a similar story. The opposition's claim that restriction equals death is not merely exaggerated; it is refuted by the evidence.

**Fourth, legalization does not reduce abortions — it multiplies them.** This is perhaps the most inconvenient truth for the pro-legalization camp. Every country that has liberalized its laws has seen the total number of abortions rise, often dramatically, as the procedure becomes normalized, destigmatized, and marketed. What was once a tragedy becomes a transaction. What was once rare becomes routine. The result is not fewer abortions performed more safely; it is vastly more abortions performed more casually. The unborn death toll multiplies, and over time, the cultural reverence for life itself erodes. Nigeria, in exchange for a contested and likely modest reduction in one category of maternal deaths, would purchase an enormous increase in the deliberate destruction of its own children. This is not a humanitarian trade; it is a moral catastrophe dressed in the language of public health.

**Fifth, the compassionate path is the one Nigeria is already pledged to walk: save both mother and child.** Nigeria should redouble its investments in maternal healthcare, expand access to skilled birth attendants, strengthen primary health centers, train more obstetricians and midwives, subsidize antenatal care, and support pregnant women economically and socially. This is the proven formula. It saves mothers without sacrificing their children. It honors the dignity of both lives. And it requires no concession whatsoever to the lethal logic that a woman's safety demands her child's death.

The maternal mortality argument, then, is not a trump card. It is a bluff — emotionally powerful, statistically inflated, logically flawed, and empirically refuted. Nigerians must see through it.

## VII. The Moral Duty to Protect the Defenseless

At the heart of any just society lies a simple principle: the strong must not be permitted to destroy the weak. But compassion does not require killing the innocent. The answer to a woman in crisis is to lift her up, not to end the life of her child. A civilization is measured not by how it treats the powerful but by how it shelters the powerless.

## VIII. Conclusion

Nigeria stands at a crossroads. Foreign lobbying, international donor pressure, inflated statistics, and shifting global fashions urge us to abandon our protections for the unborn. We must refuse. Our Criminal Code and Penal Code did not criminalize abortion by accident; our churches and mosques did not condemn it by whim; our ancestors did not cherish the womb by superstition; our demographic destiny does not call for self-destruction; our sovereignty is not a commodity to be traded for foreign approval; and the genuine tragedy of maternal mortality in Nigeria demands real solutions, not the false promise that killing the unborn will save their mothers. These convergent witnesses — legal, religious, cultural, demographic, national, empirical, and moral — point to one conclusion: abortion is the taking of an innocent human life, and a just Nigeria will continue to say so.

To uphold the criminalization of abortion is not cruelty; it is fidelity — fidelity to our laws, to our faith, to our heritage, to our future, to the truth, and to the voiceless child whose only hope of protection lies in the conscience of a nation willing to stand for life.

Section 232 criminalizes causing a miscarriage, while Section 233 imposes even harsher penalties where the woman is "quick with child." Section 235 punishes those who cause the death of a quick unborn child by acts aimed at the mother. Section 236 addresses death caused to an unborn child by acts intended to prevent its being born alive.

Together, these provisions form a consistent legal architecture: the Nigerian state, through both colonial-inherited and post-independence enactments, has treated the unborn as a life deserving of criminal-law protection. Only where the life of the mother is in genuine jeopardy does the law recognize a narrow exception. This is not an accident of legislation — it is a deliberate moral choice, reaffirmed by successive generations of lawmakers. To decriminalize abortion would be to tear out a thread woven deeply into the fabric of Nigerian jurisprudence.

## II. Our Religious Heritage Affirms the Sanctity of Life

Nigeria is one of the most devout nations on earth. Both Christianity and Islam — the faiths of the overwhelming majority of our people — teach unambiguously that human life begins at conception and that the deliberate destruction of an unborn child is a grave moral wrong.

The Christian tradition draws from Scripture passages such as Psalm 139:13 — "For You formed my inward parts; You wove me in my mother's womb" — and Jeremiah 1:5 — "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you." These verses present the unborn not as clusters of tissue but as persons known and loved by God before birth. The Catholic Church, the Anglican Communion, and virtually every major Nigerian Christian denomination teach that abortion is a violation of the Fifth Commandment.

Islamic teaching, grounded in the Qur'an and Hadith, likewise condemns the killing of the unborn. Qur'an 17:31 warns: "Do not kill your children for fear of poverty. We shall provide for them and for you. Killing them is a great sin." Classical Islamic jurisprudence, across the Maliki school predominant in northern Nigeria, strongly restricts abortion, particularly after ensoulment, and treats it as a violation of a life under divine protection.

When two faiths that shape the moral compass of more than 95% of Nigerians speak with one voice, the state has every reason — and every right — to reflect that moral consensus in its laws.

## III. African Cultural Values Honor the Child in the Womb

Long before colonial statutes, African tradition treated pregnancy as sacred. Among the Igbo, the Yoruba, the Hausa-Fulani, the Tiv, the Ijaw, and countless other peoples, an unborn child is regarded as a gift from God and the ancestors — a future member of the community whose arrival is anticipated with joy, ritual, and prayer. Naming ceremonies, prenatal blessings, and communal protection of pregnant women all testify to a worldview in which the child in the womb is already *someone*, already a relation, already belonging.

To speak of "discarding" such a life is alien to our cultural imagination. Traditional African ethics — often summed up in the principle of *ubuntu*, "I am because we are" — insists that every human being is constituted by relationships, including the relationship between mother and unborn child. To legalize abortion is to import a foreign individualism that treats the womb as private property rather than a sacred trust. Nigeria must not allow external pressures, no matter how well-funded or well-branded, to erode a cultural inheritance that has sustained our peoples for millennia.

## IV. The Unborn and the Future of the Nigerian Nation

Beyond the moral and spiritual dimensions lies a stark demographic and developmental truth: **every abortion is the loss of a future Nigerian**. By 2050, the United Nations projects that Nigeria will become the third most populous nation on earth, surpassing the United States. While some voices frame this as a crisis, visionary leadership recognizes it for what it truly is: an extraordinary opportunity. Our youth are, and will remain, our single greatest national resource.

Consider what every terminated pregnancy actually represents. It is not an abstraction. It is a future doctor who will never treat patients in Enugu. It is a future agricultural scientist who will never help feed the Sahel. It is a future engineer who will never design the bridges of Lagos, a future teacher who will never stand before a classroom in Kano, a future entrepreneur who will never build the company that employs thousands. It is a future poet, a future judge, a future mother, a future president. Each of these lives carries within it an entire world of possibility — possibility that abortion extinguishes forever.

Nations that have embraced abortion on demand are today confronting the consequences of their choices: collapsing birthrates, aging populations, shrinking workforces, and economies straining under the weight of demographic decline. Japan, much of Europe, and parts of East Asia now spend enormous sums trying to reverse the very trend they once celebrated. Nigeria need not repeat their mistake. Our youthful population is not a burden to be managed through population-control programs; it is the engine of our coming prosperity — provided we have the wisdom to let it live.

To permit widespread abortion is to inflict a self-inflicted wound on the Nigerian future. It is to trade generations of talent for the fleeting convenience of the present. A nation serious about development invests in its children, born and unborn, through maternal healthcare, economic support for families, education, and opportunity — not through the elimination of the next generation before it draws breath.

## V. Nigerian Sovereignty and the Foreign Agenda

It must be said plainly: the push to liberalize Nigeria's abortion laws is not, in the main, rising from the Nigerian grassroots. Poll after poll confirms that Nigerians — across regions, religions, and ethnic groups — overwhelmingly oppose abortion on demand. So where is the pressure coming from? Increasingly, it comes from well-funded international organizations such as the **International Planned Parenthood Federation (IPPF)**, **MSI Reproductive Choices (formerly Marie Stopes International)**, and a constellation of Western donor agencies and foundations that channel vast sums into "reproductive rights" advocacy across Africa.

These organizations operate with budgets that dwarf those of local Nigerian civil society groups. They fund workshops, train journalists, draft model legislation, sponsor "sensitization" campaigns, and cultivate relationships with lawmakers and ministries. Their framing — the language of "safe abortion," "reproductive justice," "unmet need" — is carefully crafted to make the destruction of unborn life sound like healthcare and to make Nigeria's longstanding moral consensus sound like backwardness.

This is not partnership; it is a soft form of ideological colonialism. The same Western capitals that once imposed their commerce and their borders upon Africa now seek to impose their sexual and demographic orthodoxies. It is telling that while these organizations lecture African nations on "choice," their home governments simultaneously worry about their own falling birthrates. The question Nigerians must ask is simple: *Why are the people most concerned about African population growth not Africans themselves?*

Nigerian sovereignty demands that our laws reflect Nigerian values, debated by Nigerian citizens, enacted by Nigerian legislators, and rooted in Nigerian moral tradition — not conditions attached to foreign aid, not templates written in London or New York, and not the policy preferences of organizations whose ideological commitments predate and override any genuine concern for African flourishing. To yield on abortion is to concede that our moral vision is negotiable in exchange for foreign approval. It is not.

## VI. The Maternal Mortality Myth: Confronting the Opposition's Trump Card

No argument is deployed more often or more emotionally in favor of liberalization than the claim that Nigeria must legalize abortion to save women's lives. The figure most commonly cited — roughly 6,000 maternal deaths per year attributed to "unsafe abortion," drawn from Guttmacher Institute estimates — is presented as though it settles the debate. It does not. When examined honestly, this argument collapses under the weight of evidence, logic, and international comparison.

**First, the real drivers of maternal mortality in Nigeria are not restrictive abortion laws.** According to the World Health Organization and Nigeria's own Federal Ministry of Health, the leading causes of maternal death in this country are postpartum hemorrhage, eclampsia and pre-eclampsia, obstructed labor, puerperal sepsis, and complications of unsafe delivery — driven by the chronic shortage of skilled birth attendants, the scarcity of functional primary health centers, inadequate emergency obstetric care, poor rural transport, and persistent poverty. These are the true killers of Nigerian mothers. To pretend that changing the abortion statute would meaningfully address them is to misdiagnose the disease and prescribe the wrong medicine. The scalpel to Nigerian maternal mortality is investment in hospitals, midwives, ambulances, blood banks, and antenatal care — not the legalization of killing the unborn.

**Second, the "unsafe abortion" figures themselves must be scrutinized with care.** Many of the most widely circulated numbers originate from advocacy organizations with an explicit policy agenda. They rely on modeling assumptions, extrapolations, and self-serving definitions in which any complication following an abortion attempt is counted as proof that restriction itself is the cause. This is circular reasoning: having defined the problem as "unsafe abortion," the only solution considered is legalization. But the alternative explanation — that these tragedies stem from poverty, clandestine practitioners, and health-system failure, all of which exist regardless of the legal regime — is systematically ignored.

**Third, the example of Chile shatters the legalization narrative completely.** For decades, Chile maintained one of the strictest abortion prohibitions in the world. Yet during that same period, Chile achieved one of the *lowest* maternal mortality rates in all of Latin America — lower than many countries with permissive abortion laws. How? Through sustained investment in maternal healthcare: trained birth attendants, accessible prenatal care, emergency obstetric services, and education for mothers. Chilean researchers including Dr. Elard Koch have documented that maternal mortality in Chile fell dramatically over decades *while* abortion remained illegal, demonstrating beyond reasonable dispute that the decisive variable is healthcare quality, not abortion access. Poland tells a similar story. The opposition's claim that restriction equals death is not merely exaggerated; it is refuted by the evidence.

**Fourth, legalization does not reduce abortions — it multiplies them.** This is perhaps the most inconvenient truth for the pro-legalization camp. Every country that has liberalized its laws has seen the total number of abortions rise, often dramatically, as the procedure becomes normalized, destigmatized, and marketed. What was once a tragedy becomes a transaction. What was once rare becomes routine. The result is not fewer abortions performed more safely; it is vastly more abortions performed more casually. The unborn death toll multiplies, and over time, the cultural reverence for life itself erodes. Nigeria, in exchange for a contested and likely modest reduction in one category of maternal deaths, would purchase an enormous increase in the deliberate destruction of its own children. This is not a humanitarian trade; it is a moral catastrophe dressed in the language of public health.

**Fifth, the compassionate path is the one Nigeria is already pledged to walk: save both mother and child.** Nigeria should redouble its investments in maternal healthcare, expand access to skilled birth attendants, strengthen primary health centers, train more obstetricians and midwives, subsidize antenatal care, and support pregnant women economically and socially. This is the proven formula. It saves mothers without sacrificing their children. It honors the dignity of both lives. And it requires no concession whatsoever to the lethal logic that a woman's safety demands her child's death.

The maternal mortality argument, then, is not a trump card. It is a bluff — emotionally powerful, statistically inflated, logically flawed, and empirically refuted. Nigerians must see through it.

## VII. The Scientific Truth: What Abortion Actually Destroys

Every honest debate about abortion must begin with an honest description of the unborn child. The language of the abortion industry — "products of conception," "pregnancy tissue," "contents of the uterus" — is deliberately crafted to obscure biological reality. Strip away the euphemism, and what embryology actually shows is this: from the moment of fertilization, a new, genetically distinct, living human organism exists, developing on its own internal timetable toward the same maturity the rest of us have reached.

**At 3 weeks after conception**, the heart begins to form and the first cardiac cells begin to pulse. **By 6 weeks**, a heartbeat can be detected on ultrasound — a rhythmic, organized beat belonging not to the mother but to a separate human being with his or her own blood type, often his or her own sex, and his or her own unique DNA. **By 8 weeks**, every major organ system has begun to form: the brain is developing distinct regions, the liver is producing blood cells, the limbs have appeared, and tiny fingers are differentiating. **By 10 weeks**, the child has measurable brainwaves. **By 12 weeks**, the unborn child has fingers and toes, responds to touch, can hiccup, swallow, and move in the womb, and possesses every internal organ in rudimentary form. **By 16 weeks**, facial expressions appear. **By 20 weeks**, a growing body of medical evidence — including research underlying fetal surgical protocols — indicates that the unborn child possesses the neurological architecture to experience pain. This is not religious speculation; it is the reason fetal anesthesia is routinely administered when surgeons operate on babies in utero. A being whose surgeons sedate him against pain is a being who can feel pain.

Against this backdrop, consider what abortion actually does. In a first-trimester suction aspiration, a powerful vacuum — many times stronger than a household appliance — tears the developing child apart and draws the remains from the womb. In a dilation and evacuation procedure, used in the second trimester, the cervix is forcibly opened and the living child is dismembered with surgical instruments, limb by limb, and removed in pieces; abortion practitioners themselves have described the need to reassemble the body parts afterward to ensure nothing is left inside the mother. In later procedures, a lethal injection may be administered directly into the child's heart to stop it before the body is extracted.

These are not pleasant facts. They are not meant to be. They are the medical reality that every honest policy debate must reckon with. A society that averts its eyes from what abortion actually is has already surrendered its capacity for moral judgment. Nigerians, who revere the sacredness of life, must not avert their eyes. The child torn apart at 12 weeks has a heartbeat, fingers, a face, and brainwaves. The child killed at 20 weeks may very well feel every instrument that ends his life. To call this "healthcare" is to corrupt the meaning of the word.

## VIII. The Moral Duty to Protect the Defenseless — and the Failure of the Autonomy Argument

At the heart of any just society lies a simple principle: the strong must not be permitted to destroy the weak. The unborn child is the weakest among us — voiceless, dependent, utterly unable to defend itself. If the law exists to protect anyone, it exists to protect those who cannot protect themselves.

Scientifically, as the previous section has shown, there is no serious dispute that human life begins at conception. From fertilization, a genetically distinct, living human organism exists, developing continuously through the stages we all once passed through. The moral line between "person" and "non-person" drawn at some arbitrary later point — viability, a trimester, birth itself — is philosophically incoherent. Either every human life has intrinsic dignity, or none of us can be sure that ours does.

This brings us to the most sophisticated argument of the pro-abortion movement: the claim of **bodily autonomy**. The argument runs that even if the unborn is a human being, a woman has the right to control her own body and therefore to end the pregnancy. This reasoning, however, collapses under minimal scrutiny.

**First, bodily autonomy has never been, and cannot be, an absolute right to kill an innocent third party.** No legal system on earth recognizes a bodily-autonomy right to commit murder, assault, or infanticide. A mother cannot invoke "my body, my choice" to kill her two-year-old child, even though caring for that child constrains her body, her time, and her freedom. The question is not whether autonomy matters — it does — but whether it extends so far as to authorize the deliberate destruction of another human being. It does not, and never has.

**Second, the unborn child is not an invader.** Except in the tragic case of rape — which accounts for a tiny fraction of abortions globally — pregnancy results from the voluntary actions of the mother and father. When two adults engage in an act whose natural and foreseeable biological consequence is the creation of a new life, they have already, by that act, accepted a duty of care toward any life so created. This is the basic logic of parental responsibility recognized by every legal system, including Nigeria's. A father cannot escape child-support obligations by saying he never intended the child; neither can a mother escape the duty not to kill her child by saying she never intended the pregnancy. To hold otherwise is to allow the author of another's vulnerability to exploit that vulnerability as grounds for destruction.

**Third, the autonomy argument proves too much.** If autonomy justifies killing the unborn at 12 weeks, why not at 30 weeks? Why not during delivery? Why not in the first hours after birth, when the infant remains as dependent on the mother's body and care as before? The defenders of abortion have no principled answer to these questions, which is why the logic of abortion on demand inexorably drifts toward later and later terminations, and in some jurisdictions, toward troubling discussions of "perinatal" decisions. The only principled stopping point is the one Nigerian law has always recognized: the inviolability of innocent human life from its beginning.

**Fourth, genuine hard cases deserve genuine compassion — not the dismantling of the entire moral order.** Arguments from poverty, unwanted pregnancy, and even rape are genuinely painful, and society owes such women compassion, comprehensive medical care, economic support, adoption services, and unrelenting justice against their attackers. But compassion does not require killing the innocent. A child conceived in tragedy is still a child; his or her life is not less valuable because of the circumstances of conception. The answer to a woman in crisis is to lift her up, not to end the life of her child. A civilization is measured not by how it treats the powerful but by how it shelters the powerless — and there are no more powerless persons on earth than the children in the womb.

## IX. Conclusion

Nigeria stands at a crossroads. Foreign lobbying, international donor pressure, inflated statistics, euphemistic language, and shifting global fashions urge us to abandon our protections for the unborn. We must refuse. Our Criminal Code and Penal Code did not criminalize abortion by accident; our churches and mosques did not condemn it by whim; our ancestors did not cherish the womb by superstition; our demographic destiny does not call for self-destruction; our sovereignty is not a commodity to be traded for foreign approval; the genuine tragedy of maternal mortality demands real solutions, not the false promise that killing the unborn will save their mothers; the science of embryology confirms beyond reasonable dispute that abortion ends a distinct human life; and no appeal to autonomy can justify the destruction of an innocent child whom the mother's own actions helped bring into being. These convergent witnesses — legal, religious, cultural, demographic, national, empirical, scientific, philosophical, and moral — point to one conclusion: abortion is the taking of an innocent human life, and a just Nigeria will continue to say so.

To uphold the criminalization of abortion is not cruelty; it is fidelity — fidelity to our laws, to our faith, to our heritage, to our future, to the truth, to the evidence of our own eyes, and to the voiceless child whose only hope of protection lies in the conscience of a nation willing to stand for life.

The first link in the chain is our own law, which speaks with unmistakable clarity. In dilation and evacuation — used in the second trimester — the cervix is forced open and the living child is dismembered with surgical instruments, limb by limb, and extracted in pieces that the practitioner must reassemble to ensure nothing remains inside the mother. In later procedures, a lethal injection is driven into the child's heart to stop it before the body is removed.

These are not agreeable facts. They are not meant to be. They are the medical truth that every honest policy debate must confront. A society that refuses to look at what abortion actually is has already forfeited its moral vision. Nigerians — who still honor life — must look, and must see. The child torn apart at 12 weeks has a beating heart, fingers, a face, brainwaves. The child killed at 20 weeks may well feel every instrument that ends his life. To call this "healthcare" is to corrupt the meaning of the word.

And once we have seen the truth, we must also defeat the final argument raised to excuse it.

## VIII. The Moral Duty to Protect the Defenseless — and the Collapse of the Autonomy Argument

At the heart of every just society lies a single principle: the strong must not be allowed to destroy the weak. The unborn child is the weakest among us — voiceless, dependent, utterly unable to defend himself. If the law exists to protect anyone, it exists to protect him.

The philosophical line that the pro-abortion movement tries to draw — separating "human being" from "person" at viability, or a trimester, or birth — is arbitrary and incoherent. Either every human life has intrinsic dignity, or none of us has any certainty our own is safe. The only principled line is the biological one: a new human life begins at conception, and from that moment forward it must be protected.

This brings us to the last and most sophisticated argument of the abortion lobby: **bodily autonomy**. Even granting that the unborn is human, they claim, a woman has the right to control her body and therefore to end the pregnancy. The argument is elegant. It is also indefensible.

**First, bodily autonomy has never included a right to kill an innocent third party.** No legal system on earth recognizes it. A mother cannot invoke "my body, my choice" to kill her two-year-old, though that child's existence constrains her body, her time, and her freedom every day. Autonomy matters — but it has never extended to the deliberate destruction of another human being, and it cannot begin to extend that far now.

**Second, the unborn child is not an intruder.** Apart from the tragedy of rape — which accounts for a small fraction of abortions — pregnancy is the natural and foreseeable consequence of the voluntary actions of two adults. When those adults engage in an act whose biological purpose is the creation of new life, they have already assumed a duty of care toward any life so created. This is the bedrock logic of parental responsibility in every legal order, including our own. A father cannot escape child support by claiming he never meant to conceive; a mother cannot escape the duty not to kill her child by claiming the same. To hold otherwise is to permit the author of another's vulnerability to cite that very vulnerability as grounds for her destruction.

**Third, the autonomy argument proves too much.** If autonomy justifies killing at 12 weeks, why not 30? Why not during labor? Why not in the first dependent hours after birth? The defenders of abortion have no principled answer — which is precisely why abortion law in permissive jurisdictions drifts steadily later, and why some now openly contemplate what they call "perinatal" decisions. The only principled stopping point is the one Nigerian law has always held: innocent human life, from its beginning, is inviolable.

**Fourth, the hard cases demand compassion — not the collapse of the moral order.** Poverty, unwanted pregnancy, even assault: these are real and grievous burdens, and society owes such women full-hearted support — medical care, economic aid, adoption services, and unrelenting justice against their attackers. But compassion never requires the killing of an innocent. A child conceived in tragedy is still a child. His life is not worth less because of how it began. The answer to a woman in crisis is to lift her up — not to end the life of her child.

A civilization is measured by how it shelters the powerless. And there are no more powerless persons on earth than the children of the womb.

## IX. Conclusion: A Clarion Call to the Nigerian Nation

The case is complete. The chain is unbroken. Our law, our faith, our culture, our future, our sovereignty, the medical evidence, the scientific reality, and the unshakable demands of moral reason — all converge on a single verdict. Abortion is the taking of an innocent human life. Nigeria has always known this. Nigeria must never forget it.

This is the hour of decision, and this is a call to action.

**To the lawmakers of the Federal Republic:** hold the line, and advance it. The existing provisions of the Criminal Code and the Penal Code must not be diluted by a single syllable. They must be strengthened — closed against loopholes, enforced without fear, and defended against every foreign-funded campaign that seeks to erode them. Legislate for life. Resist the NGOs, the donor conditions, the model bills, and the seductive vocabulary of "reform." Your oath is to the Nigerian people — including the smallest and most silent of them.

**To the religious leaders of every pulpit, mosque, and altar:** preach. Preach without apology the sanctity of life from conception. Teach your congregations. Form your young people. Challenge your politicians. You are the conscience of this nation, and the nation needs that conscience loud and clear.

**To the doctors, nurses, and medical students of Nigeria:** honor your oath. Refuse to participate in abortion. Refuse to let foreign "training" programs turn your healing hands into instruments of death. Invest your gifts where they are desperately needed — in saving Nigerian mothers and delivering Nigerian children, the calling for which medicine exists.

**To the journalists and educators of Nigeria:** tell the truth. Show the science. Name the funders. Refuse the euphemisms. The Nigerian people deserve to know what abortion is, who is pushing it, and why.

**To every Nigerian citizen:** the defense of the unborn is not someone else's battle. It is yours. Speak in your family. Vote in your ward. Support pregnant women in crisis. Volunteer for life-affirming causes. Refuse the lie that compassion requires killing. Insist that Nigerian values remain Nigerian law.

Our Criminal Code and Penal Code were not written by accident. Our churches and mosques do not speak by whim. Our ancestors did not honor the womb by superstition. Our demographic destiny does not call for self-destruction. Our sovereignty is not a commodity. Our mothers deserve genuine healthcare, not lethal substitutes. The science is settled. The moral truth is clear.

**Nigeria must stand for life — now, unequivocally, and always.**

To uphold the criminalization of abortion is not cruelty. It is fidelity — fidelity to our laws, to our faith, to our heritage, to our future, to the truth, to the evidence of our own eyes, and above all to the voiceless child whose only hope of protection lies in the conscience of a nation willing to rise and defend him.

Let us be that nation. Let this be that hour.
