# In Defense of Life: Why Nigeria Must Maintain the Criminalization of Abortion

Nigeria stands at a moral crossroads where the voices calling for liberalized abortion laws grow louder by the day, often echoing foreign ideologies that contradict the very foundations of our society. Yet the truth remains unshaken: the criminalization of abortion in Nigeria is not an outdated relic to be discarded, but a vital legal and moral safeguard that protects the most vulnerable members of our society—the unborn. Nigeria must hold firm to this position, rooted as it is in our law, our faith, our culture, our Constitution, and the universal principle of the sanctity of human life.

## The Law Speaks Clearly

Nigerian law has long recognized that the life of the unborn deserves protection. Sections 228, 229, and 230 of the Criminal Code Act, which governs southern Nigeria, criminalize the administration of drugs or use of instruments to procure a miscarriage, prescribing up to fourteen years imprisonment for offenders. Similarly, Sections 232 to 236 of the Penal Code, applicable in northern Nigeria, prohibit the causing of miscarriage except to save the life of the mother. These are not accidental provisions. They reflect a deliberate legislative judgment—upheld across generations—that the unborn child possesses a life worthy of the state's protection. To decriminalize abortion would be to overturn a coherent legal tradition that has served as a moral compass for our nation since independence. Laws are not mere technicalities; they are the written conscience of a people. To dismantle them would be to declare that Nigeria's conscience has changed—when, in truth, it has not.

## Our Religious Heritage Demands It

Nigeria is among the most religious nations on earth, and both Christianity and Islam—the faiths that guide the overwhelming majority of our citizens—speak with one voice on this matter. The Bible proclaims in Psalm 139:13, "For you created my inmost being; you knit me together in my mother's womb," affirming that life in the womb is divinely formed and known to God. Jeremiah 1:5 reinforces this: "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you." The Christian tradition has, for two millennia, condemned abortion as the taking of innocent life.

Islam is equally clear. The Qur'an teaches in Surah Al-Isra 17:31, "Do not kill your children for fear of poverty. We shall provide for them and for you." Islamic jurisprudence overwhelmingly holds that once ensoulment occurs, abortion is haram except to save the mother's life. For a nation where faith is not private decoration but the heartbeat of daily existence, abandoning these teachings in our laws would be a betrayal of who we are.

## Our Culture Cherishes the Child

Beyond religion, African—and specifically Nigerian—culture has always cherished children as blessings, as the future of the community, as the living bridge between ancestors and descendants. Across Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa, Ijaw, Tiv, Efik, and countless other Nigerian peoples, a pregnancy is celebrated; a child is welcomed not merely by parents but by an entire extended family and community. The naming ceremony, the rituals of welcome, the communal joy at a newborn's arrival—these are not superficial customs. They testify to a deeply held conviction that life is sacred and every child belongs to us all. To normalize abortion is to import an alien individualism that rejects this communal embrace of life.

## The Slippery Slope and the Lessons of Other Nations

We need not speculate about what decriminalization would bring; the evidence is already before our eyes. In the United States, following *Roe v. Wade* in 1973, abortion rates climbed into the millions, with over 60 million unborn children lost in the decades that followed. What began as a narrow legal exception swelled into abortion-on-demand, extending in some states even to late-term pregnancies. In parts of Europe—Sweden, the United Kingdom, France—abortion has become so normalized that it is treated as a routine procedure, stripped of the moral weight such a grave act demands. Alongside this liberalization came the collapse of birth rates below replacement level, the weakening of marriage and family bonds, and an aging population that now struggles to sustain itself. These nations are reaping what they have sown: demographic winter, economic strain, and societies increasingly detached from the generative purpose of family life.

The slippery slope is not a rhetorical device—it is a documented pattern. Once a society accepts that the unborn child may be destroyed for convenience, the protective fence around human life begins to fall in other areas. Euthanasia has now been legalized in the Netherlands, Belgium, Canada, and elsewhere, with ever-expanding criteria that now include the mentally ill, the disabled, and in some documented cases, minors. In the Netherlands, infants born with severe disabilities have been subjected to "end-of-life" protocols under the Groningen Protocol—a chilling reality that would have been unthinkable a generation ago. This is what happens when a society steps onto the road of devaluing life: it does not stop at the womb. It proceeds to the sickbed, to the nursing home, to the disabled child. Nigeria must refuse that first fatal step.

## Resisting Cultural Imperialism

This brings us to an uncomfortable but necessary truth: the push to decriminalize abortion in Nigeria is not arising organically from the Nigerian people. It is being driven by well-funded foreign organizations—IPAS, Marie Stopes International, International Planned Parenthood Federation, and their local affiliates—who pour resources into lobbying our lawmakers, training our health workers in abortion procedures, and shaping public discourse through media campaigns. Under the banner of "reproductive rights" and "safe abortion access," these groups promote an agenda crafted in Western capitals, not in Lagos, Kano, Enugu, or Port Harcourt.

This is nothing less than a new form of cultural imperialism. Having secularized their own societies and reaped bitter demographic consequences, Western donors now seek to export their ideology to Africa—and specifically to Nigeria, Africa's most populous nation and its beating heart. They dress their mission in the language of health and rights, but the underlying message is that Nigerian values are backward, that our religious convictions are obstacles to progress, and that our laws must be rewritten in the image of post-Christian Europe. We must reject this patronizing assault on our sovereignty. Nigeria's laws must be made by Nigerians, reflecting Nigerian values, not dictated by the donor agendas of Geneva, London, or New York.

## Dismantling the "Unsafe Abortion" Smokescreen

The most emotionally charged argument deployed by abortion advocates is the claim that criminalization causes women to die from "unsafe abortions." We are told, citing figures from the Guttmacher Institute and its partners, that over 1.25 million abortions occur in Nigeria annually, and that thousands of women perish each year as a result. These statistics are then wielded like a hammer to bludgeon policymakers and guilt-trip the Nigerian public into believing that only decriminalization can save women's lives. But this narrative must be examined carefully, for it is not the objective science it pretends to be.

First, the numbers themselves are deeply questionable. Guttmacher's figures are not derived from hospital records or verified national data; they are extrapolations built on surveys and modeling assumptions, many commissioned or co-produced by the very organizations advocating for decriminalization. This is a textbook conflict of interest: the researchers producing the statistics are aligned with, and in some cases funded by, the same networks lobbying for policy change. When advocacy groups generate their own evidence to support their own policy demands, the result is not neutral science—it is activism dressed in the language of data. Nigerian policymakers should treat such figures with the skepticism they deserve.

Second, and more importantly, the logical leap from "unsafe abortions occur" to "therefore abortion must be legalized" is fundamentally flawed. The real question is whether criminalization is the cause of maternal death, and the international evidence decisively says no. Consider Poland, which maintains one of the strictest abortion laws in Europe and yet enjoys a maternal mortality rate far lower than many countries with liberal abortion regimes. Consider Malta, which prohibits abortion entirely and has one of the lowest maternal mortality rates in the world. Consider the Philippines, a Catholic nation with strict abortion laws that has continued to reduce maternal deaths through improved obstetric care, not by legalizing abortion. Meanwhile, countries with permissive abortion laws but weak healthcare systems continue to record high maternal mortality. The correlation is unmistakable: what kills women is not the law, but the quality of healthcare available to them.

This truth exposes the "unsafe abortion" argument for what it is—a smokescreen. If abortion advocates genuinely cared about maternal health, they would be pouring their resources into training midwives, equipping rural clinics, combating postpartum hemorrhage, fighting eclampsia, improving emergency obstetric services, and expanding skilled birth attendance across Nigeria. These are the real killers of Nigerian mothers: hemorrhage, sepsis, hypertensive disorders, obstructed labor, and unsafe deliveries—not the restriction of abortion. Yet the foreign NGOs pushing decriminalization devote disproportionate energy to one narrow cause: making abortion legal and accessible. This selective focus reveals their priorities. The goal is not saving lives; the goal is ideological conformity.

There is also a deeper moral incoherence in the "unsafe abortion" argument. It essentially says: "Because some people break the law and harm themselves in the process, the law must be abolished." By this reasoning, we should legalize armed robbery because armed robbers sometimes get shot, or legalize drug trafficking because traffickers sometimes die in the trade. We do not abolish laws against grave moral wrongs simply because those who defy them face consequences. The answer to illegal abortion is not legal abortion—it is stronger families, stronger communities, better support for mothers in crisis, adoption alternatives, and a culture that welcomes every child. The solution to the genuine tragedy of maternal death is better obstetric care, universal health coverage, and public investment in women's health—not the legalization of the very act that kills the unborn.

Nigeria must refuse the false choice between dead mothers and dead babies. We can and must save both. That is the path of true compassion.

## Nigeria's Demographic Destiny

Nigeria is projected to become the third most populous nation on earth by 2050, with a young, dynamic population poised to drive Africa's economic rise. This is not a crisis to be managed by reducing births through liberalized abortion—it is a gift to be nurtured. Our children are our greatest natural resource, more valuable than our oil, our gas, or our minerals. A nation that learns to kill its own future in the womb has no future at all. We must invest in mothers, in families, in healthcare, and in education—not in the destruction of the very generation that will carry Nigeria forward.

## The Unborn Child Is a Person: The Constitutional and Scientific Case

We now arrive at the heart of the matter, the argument from which all others draw their strength: the unborn child is a human person, and as such, is entitled to the full protection of the Nigerian Constitution. Every other consideration—law, religion, culture, public health—rests upon this foundational truth, and it is here that the case for criminalization becomes not merely persuasive but inescapable.

Section 33(1) of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria, 1999, declares in plain and unequivocal terms: **"Every person has a right to life, and no one shall be deprived intentionally of his life, save in execution of the sentence of a court in respect of a criminal offence of which he has been found guilty in Nigeria."** This is the supreme law of the land. It admits no exceptions for age, size, stage of development, dependency, or location. The right to life extends to every person—and the only question that remains is whether the unborn child is a person.

Modern embryology answers this question with absolute clarity, free from religious or political coloring. From the moment of fertilization, a new, genetically distinct human organism comes into existence. The single-celled zygote contains a complete and unique human genome—46 chromosomes, half from each parent, forming a DNA sequence that has never existed before and will never exist again. This is not the mother's body; it is not a "clump of cells"; it is not a "potential human being." It is a human being at the earliest stage of his or her development. By day 22 after fertilization, the tiny heart begins to beat—a heart that will, if unharmed, continue beating for 70, 80, or 100 years. By six weeks, measurable brain activity is recorded. By eight weeks, every major organ system is present in rudimentary form. By twenty weeks—and many scientists now argue considerably earlier—the unborn child possesses the neurological architecture necessary to perceive pain, recoiling from harmful stimuli in ways that have moved many in the medical community to insist on fetal anesthesia during prenatal surgery.

These are not theological claims. They are findings printed in every standard embryology textbook used in medical schools around the world, including those used to train Nigerian doctors. Keith Moore's *The Developing Human*, Langman's *Medical Embryology*, and Sadler's authoritative works all affirm that human life begins at fertilization. To deny this is not to exercise skepticism—it is to deny science itself.

The syllogism, then, is airtight:
- **Premise 1:** Every person has a constitutional right to life under Section 33(1).
- **Premise 2:** The unborn child is, from fertilization, a living human being—a person in the fullest biological sense.
- **Conclusion:** The unborn child possesses a constitutional right to life, and abortion is a violation of that right.

This is not abstraction. This is the logical and legal reality. Abortion, under the Nigerian Constitution rightly understood, is not a "right" at all. It is state-permitted killing of the most vulnerable class of Nigerians—citizens who cannot speak, cannot vote, cannot flee, and cannot defend themselves. If Section 33(1) means anything, it must mean that the state cannot abandon these smallest Nigerians to destruction in the name of convenience, economics, or foreign ideology. Any legal regime that fails to protect them is constitutionally incoherent.

### The Bodily Autonomy Fallacy

It is here that advocates of abortion raise their final and most seductive argument: the claim of "bodily autonomy." A woman, they say, has the right to do what she wishes with her own body, and abortion is a matter of that right. This argument is pronounced with the confidence of a self-evident truth. But it collapses under the slightest scrutiny.

First, no one in any civilized legal system enjoys absolute bodily autonomy. The law rightly restricts what we may do with our bodies in countless ways—we cannot use our hands to strike the innocent, our vehicles to endanger others, or our tongues to incite murder. Autonomy ends where another person's right to life begins. This is the most basic principle of any legal order. To elevate bodily autonomy above the right to life is to invert the entire hierarchy of human rights, placing preference above existence.

Second—and decisively—the unborn child is **not** part of the mother's body. This is the greatest factual error at the heart of the bodily autonomy argument. Biologically, the child has his or her own distinct DNA, often a different blood type from the mother, and frequently a different sex. The child's body is genetically separate, developmentally independent in its programming, and, while dependent on the mother for sustenance, is unmistakably another person occupying the mother's womb. The uterus is not an organ of the child; the child is not an organ of the mother. A woman's body ends where her child's body begins—and the two, though connected, are never the same.

Thus, abortion is not a woman exercising control over her own body. It is the ending of another human being's life using the mother's body as the site of that killing. No coherent understanding of autonomy extends to the deliberate destruction of a separate human being. We would never accept the argument that a mother has "autonomy" to kill her infant after birth because the infant, too, depends on her for survival. Dependency does not diminish personhood, and location in the womb does not strip a human being of the right to life. A journey of nine inches down the birth canal does not transform a non-person into a person. Either the being in the womb is human, or he or she is not—and science has settled that question beyond reasonable dispute.

The bodily autonomy argument, therefore, is not an argument at all. It is a rhetorical device that depends on obscuring the humanity of the unborn child. Once that humanity is recognized—as our Constitution, our science, our laws, our faiths, and our culture all insist it must be—the argument disintegrates. There is no right to kill another human being for one's own convenience. There never has been, and there never will be.

## The Sanctity of Life Is Non-Negotiable

At the heart of the matter lies a simple, inescapable truth: the unborn child is a human life. From conception, a unique genetic identity exists—distinct, growing, and destined, if unharmed, to be born. To terminate that life is to take a human life. No euphemism about "choice" or "reproductive rights" can dissolve this biological reality. A just society is measured by how it treats its weakest members, and none are weaker or more defenseless than the child in the womb, who has no voice, no vote, and no means to plead for mercy.

## Conclusion

Nigeria must not yield to pressures—foreign or domestic—to decriminalize abortion. Our laws enshrine protection for the unborn; our faiths demand it; our cultures celebrate it; the evidence of decayed societies warns against it; the manipulative statistics of foreign NGOs cannot deceive us; our Constitution, read honestly alongside modern embryology, requires the protection of every Nigerian life from fertilization; and the sanctity of life demands nothing less. To abandon the criminalization of abortion would be to abandon a piece of our soul—to surrender our smallest citizens to destruction and our future to those who do not share our values. Let Nigeria stand firm, defending life from conception, resisting the siren songs of cultural imperialism, investing in genuine maternal health, honoring our Constitution, and remaining true to the moral inheritance that defines us. The unborn child is one of us. She is a Nigerian. She has a name yet unspoken, a future yet unlived, and a right to life written into the supreme law of our land. Let us have the courage to protect her.

- Therefore the unborn child possesses the constitutional right to life, and abortion is a constitutional violation.

Abortion is not a "right." It is the state-permitted killing of the most defenseless Nigerians—citizens who cannot speak, cannot vote, cannot flee, cannot plead. Any legal regime that denies them Section 33(1) is constitutionally incoherent. Either the Constitution means what it says, or it means nothing.

### The Bodily Autonomy Argument Collapses

The abortion lobby retreats to one last slogan: bodily autonomy. The claim is pronounced as self-evident. It is not. It is false in principle and false in fact.

It is false in principle because no legal system anywhere grants absolute bodily autonomy. We cannot use our bodies to strike the innocent, to endanger others, or to take another life. Autonomy ends where another person's right to life begins. To elevate autonomy above life is to invert every moral and legal hierarchy human civilization has ever built.

It is false in fact because the unborn child is not part of the mother's body. The child possesses distinct DNA, often a different blood type, and frequently a different sex. The uterus is not an organ of the child; the child is not an organ of the mother. They are two persons, connected for a season, never identical. Abortion is therefore not a woman disposing of her own body; it is the ending of another human being's life within her body. Dependency does not erase personhood—newborns, the elderly, the sick all depend on others, yet none forfeit the right to life thereby. Location does not erase personhood—a journey of nine inches through the birth canal cannot transform a non-person into a person. Either the being in the womb is human, or she is not. Science has answered. The argument is over.

The bodily autonomy claim survives only by concealing the humanity of the child. Once that humanity is seen, the argument dissolves. There is no right to kill an innocent human being for one's convenience. There never was. There never will be.

## The Call to Action

To the lawmakers of the Federal Republic of Nigeria: you swore an oath to uphold the Constitution. Section 33(1) binds you. Every vote to loosen abortion restrictions is a vote against the supreme law you swore to defend. Stand firm. Strengthen the Criminal Code and the Penal Code. Reject every bill, every amendment, every "policy guideline" that advances the foreign agenda of abortion access. Your names will be remembered either among those who guarded the weakest Nigerians or among those who sold them.

To the religious leaders of Nigeria—bishops, imams, pastors, sheikhs, clerics of every tradition: your pulpits shape the nation's conscience. Preach this truth without apology. Teach your congregations that the unborn child is their neighbor, their fellow Nigerian, their brother and sister made in the image of God. Do not be silenced by the language of rights wielded to defend a wrong. The prophets did not flinch. Neither must you.

To the mothers, fathers, doctors, nurses, lawyers, journalists, students, and citizens of Nigeria: this is your fight. The campaign to decriminalize abortion depends on your silence. Break it. Speak in your homes, your markets, your classrooms, your hospitals, your courtrooms, your timelines. Support the mother in crisis. Open your home to the child without parents. Refuse the lie that Nigerian progress requires Nigerian abortion. Progress built on the corpses of children is not progress—it is decay wearing progress as a mask.

Nigeria, the hour is now. Our laws command us. Our faiths command us. Our cultures command us. The failures of other nations warn us. The manipulations of foreign donors insult us. Our Constitution, joined to the unassailable findings of modern science, binds us. The unborn child is one of us. She is a Nigerian. She has a name yet unspoken, a future yet unlived, and a right to life written into the supreme law of the land. Defend her. Let no generation of Nigerians ever be ashamed of what we did in this hour—let every generation thereafter rise up and call us blessed for refusing to betray her.

# In Defense of Life: Why Nigeria Must Maintain and Enforce the Criminalization of Abortion

Nigeria stands at a moral crossroads where the pressure of foreign ideologies, well-funded international lobbies, and shifting global trends threatens to erode one of the most fundamental convictions of our society: the sanctity of human life from conception. Against this tide, Nigeria must hold firm. The criminalization of abortion, enshrined in Sections 228, 229, and 230 of our Criminal Code and Sections 232–234 of the Penal Code, is not an outdated relic to be discarded but a principled legal stance that protects the most vulnerable members of our society — the unborn. These laws must not only be maintained but rigorously enforced.

## The Unborn Child Is a Human Being With a Right to Life

The foundation of any just legal system is the protection of human life. Scientifically, biologically, and incontrovertibly, human life begins at conception. At the moment of fertilization, a new organism with distinct human DNA — separate from both mother and father — comes into being. This is not metaphysical speculation; it is embryology. To argue that this human being is not yet "a person" is to engage in arbitrary line-drawing that has historically been used to deny rights to entire classes of people. If personhood depends on size, development, location, or dependency, then the logic threatens not only the unborn but the elderly, the disabled, and the infirm.

Section 33(1) of the Nigerian Constitution guarantees every person the right to life. If we are serious about that guarantee, we must extend it to the unborn, who are the most defenseless human beings in existence. A society that permits the deliberate destruction of its own children in the womb is a society that has lost its moral foundation.

## Our Laws Reflect — and Must Continue to Reflect — Nigerian Values

Nigeria is a deeply religious, family-centered, and morally grounded society. Across our diverse communities — Christian, Muslim, and traditional — there is a powerful and shared conviction that life is sacred and given by God. This is not accidental, and it is not something to be ashamed of. Laws must arise from the moral convictions of the people they govern, and Nigerian law rightly reflects the consensus of the vast majority of Nigerians who regard abortion as a grave moral wrong.

Western nations, having abandoned these convictions, now suffer plummeting birth rates, disintegrating family structures, and profound cultural confusion about even the most basic questions of human identity. Nigeria must resist the temptation to import these failed experiments. We are not obliged to legislate according to the moral fashions of New York or Geneva. Our sovereignty includes the right to legislate according to our own values — and our values affirm life.

## The "Safe Abortion" Argument Is a Distortion

Advocates of decriminalization often argue that criminal prohibition drives women to unsafe abortions and that legalization would save lives. This argument, though emotionally appealing, collapses under scrutiny. First, it is morally incoherent: it asks us to legalize the intentional killing of one human being in order to make that killing "safer" for the one doing it. By the same logic, we might legalize any dangerous crime to make it safer for the perpetrator.

Second, the real solutions to maternal mortality in Nigeria lie elsewhere — in improving general healthcare access, expanding maternal care, strengthening education, supporting pregnant women in crisis, and combating the poverty that often drives desperate decisions. Legalizing abortion would not address any of these root causes; it would merely normalize the ending of innocent lives and treat the womb as a place of danger rather than nurture.

Third, countries that have legalized abortion have not seen it remain "rare." They have seen it become routine, industrialized, and entrenched. Once the legal principle is surrendered, there is no natural stopping point.

## Protecting Women Means Protecting Mothers and Children Together

A truly pro-woman society does not offer abortion as a solution to hardship; it offers support, dignity, and community. The assumption that women need abortion to flourish is itself a profoundly pessimistic view of womanhood — one that treats pregnancy as a disease and motherhood as an obstacle. Nigerian culture has always honored mothers, and our laws should reinforce, not undermine, this honor by ensuring that no woman feels that killing her child is her only option.

What is needed is not decriminalization but compassionate enforcement paired with robust social support: crisis pregnancy centers, adoption systems, maternal healthcare, economic support for struggling mothers, and moral formation that honors both mother and child. Criminal law sets the moral boundary; social investment provides the path forward within it.

## Enforcement Is a Matter of Justice

Laws that are not enforced are laws in name only. The current reality in Nigeria — where illegal abortions occur in clandestine clinics across our cities — is a failure not of the law itself but of enforcement. Those who perform illegal abortions, particularly the profiteering practitioners who exploit vulnerable women, must face the full weight of the law. Justice demands no less. The unborn cannot speak for themselves, cannot march in protest, cannot vote. Precisely for that reason, the state has a heightened duty to speak for them through vigorous enforcement.

## Conclusion

Nigeria must not bend to international pressure, nor to the seductive but hollow arguments of those who would redefine human life for the sake of convenience. The unborn child is a human being, fully deserving of the protection of our laws. Our Constitution affirms the right to life; our culture affirms the sanctity of life; our faith traditions affirm that life is a gift from God. The criminalization of abortion is not cruelty — it is justice. It is not backwardness — it is moral clarity in an age of confusion.

Let Nigeria stand firm. Let us maintain our laws, enforce them courageously, and build a society where every child — born and unborn — is welcomed, protected, and cherished. In defending the unborn, we defend the very principle that makes any society worth preserving: that every human life, without exception, is sacred.

China's decades of aggressive population control have left it with a demographic crisis so severe that it now frantically encourages childbirth. Europe, Japan, and South Korea face similar reckonings. These nations would give almost anything to have what Nigeria has: a young population full of promise.

To legalize abortion in Nigeria would be to voluntarily inflict on ourselves the wound that is killing other civilizations. It would mean the elimination of millions of future Nigerians — future farmers who would feed our continent, future scientists who would solve our problems, future soldiers who would defend our borders, future thinkers who would articulate a Nigerian vision for the world. Nigeria is not a secular state in the Western, anti-religious sense. This is not a matter of minor theological disagreement; it is a point of profound convergence. Any attempt to legalize abortion is therefore not a "neutral" reform but a direct assault on the shared religious conscience of the Nigerian people.

**The Christian witness is unambiguous.** The Catholic Bishops' Conference of Nigeria (CBCN) has, time and again, issued unflinching pastoral letters condemning abortion as a grave moral evil — an offense against God, against the innocent child, and against the mother herself. Our bishops have rightly called abortion what it is: the unjust taking of human life. The Catholic Church teaches, in line with two thousand years of moral tradition, that human life must be respected and protected absolutely from the moment of conception. This teaching is echoed powerfully across Nigeria's vast Protestant, Pentecostal, and Evangelical communities — from the Christian Association of Nigeria (CAN) to the Anglican Communion, the Redeemed Christian Church of God, Deeper Life, and countless others. On this question, Nigerian Christianity is united.

**The Islamic witness is equally strong.** Islamic jurisprudence, across all four major Sunni schools of law — the Maliki, Hanafi, Shafi'i, and Hanbali madhabs — condemns abortion. While classical scholars historically discussed the concept of ensoulment (nafkh al-ruh), after which all schools unanimously regard abortion as categorically forbidden and tantamount to the killing of a human soul, a great many jurists — including prominent Maliki scholars whose tradition is deeply rooted in Northern Nigeria — prohibit abortion from the very moment of conception, recognizing that the process of human formation begins immediately. The Qur'an itself declares: *"Do not kill your children for fear of poverty — We provide for them and for you. Surely the killing of them is a great sin"* (Qur'an 17:31). The protection of life (*hifz al-nafs*) is one of the foundational objectives (*maqasid*) of Shariah. For the Muslim ummah of Nigeria, the defense of the unborn is not a political posture — it is a religious duty.

When Nigerian Christians and Muslims — who so often are portrayed as divided — stand together on a question of such gravity, the nation's legislators have no legitimate ground to overturn that consensus. A government that legalizes abortion in Nigeria would be legislating against the conscience of virtually its entire citizenry, in service of a small, foreign-funded elite. That is not democracy. One of the most frequently repeated claims of the pro-abortion lobby is that "criminalization does not reduce abortion rates." This assertion, dressed up in the language of social science, is simply not true — and it deserves to be dismantled directly.

Poland, which tightened its already-strict abortion laws in recent years, likewise records abortion rates dramatically lower than those of its permissive European neighbors. Ireland, before its unfortunate 2018 referendum, had one of the lowest abortion rates in the Western world precisely because of its legal and cultural protection of the unborn. Meanwhile, countries with permissive abortion laws — the United Kingdom, France, Sweden, the United States — record vastly higher abortion rates. The correlation between legal restriction and lower abortion rates is real, observable, and persistent.

The claim that laws don't matter is, frankly, absurd on its face. We do not argue that murder laws fail to reduce murder, or that theft laws fail to reduce theft. # In Defense of Life and Nation: Why Nigeria Must Maintain and Enforce the Criminalization of Abortion

Nigeria stands at a moral and historical crossroads. The pressure of foreign ideologies, well-funded international lobbies, and shifting global trends threatens to erode one of the most fundamental convictions of our society: the sanctity of human life from conception. Against this tide, Nigeria must hold firm. The criminalization of abortion, enshrined in Sections 228, 229, and 230 of our Criminal Code and Sections 232–234 of the Penal Code, is not an outdated relic to be discarded but a principled legal stance that protects the most vulnerable members of our society — the unborn — while simultaneously safeguarding the future strength and destiny of our nation. These laws must not only be maintained but rigorously enforced.

## The Unborn Child Is a Human Being With a Right to Life

The foundation of any just legal system is the protection of human life. Scientifically, biologically, and incontrovertibly, human life begins at conception. At the moment of fertilization, a new organism with distinct human DNA — separate from both mother and father — comes into being. This is not metaphysical speculation; it is embryology. To argue that this human being is not yet "a person" is to engage in arbitrary line-drawing that has historically been used to deny rights to entire classes of people. If personhood depends on size, development, location, or dependency, then the logic threatens not only the unborn but the elderly, the disabled, and the infirm.

Section 33(1) of the Nigerian Constitution guarantees every person the right to life. If we are serious about that guarantee, we must extend it to the unborn, who are the most defenseless human beings in existence. A society that permits the deliberate destruction of its own children in the womb is a society that has lost its moral foundation.

## A Nation United in Faith: The Overwhelming Religious Consensus Against Abortion

Nigeria is not a secular state in the Western, anti-religious sense. We are a nation in which **over 95% of the population identifies as either Christian or Muslim**, and on the question of abortion, these two great traditions — which together account for the overwhelming moral imagination of our people — speak with one voice. This is not a matter of minor theological disagreement; it is a point of profound convergence. Any attempt to legalize abortion is therefore not a "neutral" reform but a direct assault on the shared religious conscience of the Nigerian people.

**The Christian witness is unambiguous.** The Catholic Bishops' Conference of Nigeria (CBCN) has, time and again, issued unflinching pastoral letters condemning abortion as a grave moral evil — an offense against God, against the innocent child, and against the mother herself. Our bishops have rightly called abortion what it is: the unjust taking of human life. The Catholic Church teaches, in line with two thousand years of moral tradition, that human life must be respected and protected absolutely from the moment of conception. This teaching is echoed powerfully across Nigeria's vast Protestant, Pentecostal, and Evangelical communities — from the Christian Association of Nigeria (CAN) to the Anglican Communion, the Redeemed Christian Church of God, Deeper Life, and countless others. On this question, Nigerian Christianity is united.

**The Islamic witness is equally strong.** Islamic jurisprudence, across all four major Sunni schools of law — the Maliki, Hanafi, Shafi'i, and Hanbali madhabs — condemns abortion. While classical scholars historically discussed the concept of ensoulment (nafkh al-ruh), after which all schools unanimously regard abortion as categorically forbidden and tantamount to the killing of a human soul, a great many jurists — including prominent Maliki scholars whose tradition is deeply rooted in Northern Nigeria — prohibit abortion from the very moment of conception, recognizing that the process of human formation begins immediately. The Qur'an itself declares: *"Do not kill your children for fear of poverty — We provide for them and for you. Surely the killing of them is a great sin"* (Qur'an 17:31). The protection of life (*hifz al-nafs*) is one of the foundational objectives (*maqasid*) of Shariah. For the Muslim ummah of Nigeria, the defense of the unborn is not a political posture — it is a religious duty.

When Nigerian Christians and Muslims — who so often are portrayed as divided — stand together on a question of such gravity, the nation's legislators have no legitimate ground to overturn that consensus. A government that legalizes abortion in Nigeria would be legislating against the conscience of virtually its entire citizenry, in service of a small, foreign-funded elite. That is not democracy. That is moral colonialism.

## Our Laws Reflect — and Must Continue to Reflect — Nigerian Values

Laws must arise from the moral convictions of the people they govern, and Nigerian law rightly reflects the consensus of the vast majority of Nigerians who regard abortion as a grave moral wrong. Western nations, having abandoned these convictions, now suffer plummeting birth rates, disintegrating family structures, and profound cultural confusion about even the most basic questions of human identity. Nigeria must resist the temptation to import these failed experiments. We are not obliged to legislate according to the moral fashions of New York or Geneva. Our sovereignty includes the right to legislate according to our own values — and our values affirm life.

## Protecting the Unborn Is Protecting Nigeria's Future Prosperity and Power

Beyond the moral imperative, there is a powerful national interest at stake: **our people are our greatest resource**. Oil wells run dry. Commodity prices fluctuate. But a young, vibrant, growing population is the most enduring foundation of national greatness. To legalize abortion would be to strike directly at the heart of Nigeria's rising power.

Nigeria is projected to become the **third most populous country in the world by 2050**, surpassing the United States. With a median age under 20, Nigeria possesses what economists call a "demographic dividend" — a vast youthful population poised to become workers, entrepreneurs, innovators, soldiers, teachers, engineers, artists, and leaders. This demographic reality is not a burden to be managed through population control; it is a strategic asset of immense value. Every nation that has risen to global prominence did so on the back of a growing, confident, productive population.

Consider what legalized abortion has done elsewhere. In country after country, tens of millions of children have been eliminated before birth. Today, those nations face demographic catastrophe: aging populations, shrinking workforces, unsustainable pension systems, and a desperate reliance on mass immigration. China's decades of aggressive population control have left it with a demographic crisis so severe that it now frantically encourages childbirth. Europe, Japan, and South Korea face similar reckonings. These nations would give almost anything to have what Nigeria has: a young population full of promise.

To legalize abortion in Nigeria would be to voluntarily inflict on ourselves the wound that is killing other civilizations. Each aborted child is a contribution to the nation forever extinguished — a teacher never trained, a business never founded, a family line permanently severed. A Nigeria of 400 million confident, educated, productive citizens will be a superpower. A Nigeria that has aborted a generation of its children will be a cautionary tale.

## The Eugenic Reality of Legal Abortion: A Weapon Against Women, the Disabled, and the Marginalized

Of all the arguments against legalizing abortion in Nigeria, none is more urgent or more damning than this: **wherever abortion has been legalized, it has been used as a tool of discrimination — targeting girls, the disabled, and ethnic minorities with devastating efficiency.** The promise of "choice" has, in practice, become the reality of eugenics. Nigeria must open its eyes to what legalized abortion has actually done in the world — not what its advocates claim it will do.

**Consider the war on girls.** In India and China, the legalization and normalization of abortion — combined with prenatal sex determination — has produced one of the great human rights catastrophes of the modern era: the systematic elimination of unborn girls on a mass scale. Demographers estimate that more than **140 million women are "missing"** from the world's population — killed in the womb simply because they were female. In parts of India, sex ratios have collapsed to fewer than 850 girls for every 1,000 boys. China's one-child policy, enforced by mass abortion, produced a generation so skewed that tens of millions of men today cannot find wives, fueling human trafficking, bride-buying, and social instability. Let us be clear: in these countries, legalized abortion did not empower women. It **annihilated** them before they drew breath. Feminism's greatest betrayal of women has been its refusal to speak honestly about this horror.

**Now consider the war on the disabled.** In Iceland, over **95% of unborn children diagnosed with Down syndrome are aborted.** In Denmark, the figure approaches 98%. In the United Kingdom, it is around 90%. These nations now openly boast of having "eliminated" Down syndrome — not by curing it, but by killing those who have it. This is eugenics in its purest and most chilling form: the systematic extermination of an entire category of human beings because society has deemed their lives less worthy of existence. The philosophy behind it is identical to the philosophy that animated the darkest chapters of the 20th century — only the methods are cleaner, and the screams quieter. If Nigeria legalizes abortion, how long before our own children with Down syndrome, with sickle cell anemia, with cleft palates, with autism, are quietly eliminated before birth? How long before prenatal "defect screening" becomes a tool for purging the weak from our society?

**Consider, too, the racial and ethnic dimension.** In the United States, Black Americans make up roughly 13% of the population — yet Black children account for over one-third of all abortions. Black women have abortion rates nearly **four times higher** than white women. This is not an accident. **Margaret Sanger**, the founder of Planned Parenthood — the organization whose "research arm" produces most of the global pro-abortion statistics cited by advocates — was an open and unapologetic eugenicist. She wrote admiringly of limiting the reproduction of those she considered "unfit," engaged with white supremacist organizations to promote birth control in Black communities through what she called the "Negro Project," and spoke openly of the need to reduce the populations of "inferior" groups. The organization she founded has, by every measurable standard, pursued exactly that legacy: today, in cities like New York, **more Black babies are aborted than are born alive.** This is not liberation. It is a slow-motion racial catastrophe, and it is precisely the future that pro-abortion advocates, knowingly or not, would import into Nigeria.

**Now consider what this would mean for Nigeria specifically.** We are a nation of extraordinary ethnic diversity — the Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa-Fulani, Ijaw, Tiv, Kanuri, Efik, Ibibio, and hundreds of other proud peoples. Cultural preferences for sons still exist in many Nigerian communities; it would be naïve to pretend otherwise. Dowry pressures, inheritance customs, and patrilineal expectations all create contexts in which legalized sex-selective abortion would flourish, as it has in India. How many Nigerian girls would never be born? How many would be eliminated simply because their parents hoped for a son? And what of our children with disabilities, in a country already struggling with stigma around disability? What of the poorer ethnic communities who, as in America, would find themselves disproportionately targeted by abortion providers setting up shop in their neighborhoods under the banner of "reproductive health"?

**Legalized abortion is not, and has never been, neutral.** It is not the gentle expansion of personal freedom that its advocates claim. It has functioned, repeatedly and predictably, as a mechanism by which the powerful eliminate those whom they find inconvenient — girls, the disabled, the poor, the ethnically marginalized. To legalize abortion in Nigeria in the name of "women's autonomy" would be to perpetrate a cruel fraud, because the principal victims of legalized abortion around the world have been **women themselves** — killed before birth in numbers that stagger the imagination — along with disabled children and minority communities.

The pro-abortion movement asks Nigeria to close its eyes to this blood-soaked global record and pretend that somehow, in our case, the outcome will be different. It will not be. The logic of abortion is always the same: once human life becomes disposable, it is always the most vulnerable who are disposed of first. And in Nigeria, those vulnerable lives would overwhelmingly be our own daughters, our own disabled children, and the children of our poorest communities.

## Dismantling the "Criminalization Doesn't Work" Myth

One of the most frequently repeated claims of the pro-abortion lobby is that "criminalization does not reduce abortion rates." This assertion, dressed up in the language of social science, is simply not true.

**Consider the actual evidence from countries with strict abortion laws.** Malta, which until recently prohibited abortion entirely and still maintains among the most restrictive laws in Europe, has one of the lowest abortion rates in the developed world. Poland, which tightened its already-strict abortion laws in recent years, likewise records abortion rates dramatically lower than those of its permissive European neighbors. Ireland, before its 2018 referendum, had one of the lowest abortion rates in the Western world precisely because of its legal and cultural protection of the unborn. Meanwhile, countries with permissive abortion laws — the United Kingdom, France, Sweden, the United States — record vastly higher abortion rates. The correlation between legal restriction and lower abortion rates is real, observable, and persistent.

The claim that laws don't matter is absurd on its face. We do not argue that murder laws fail to reduce murder, or that theft laws fail to reduce theft. Laws shape behavior because they shape culture, expectations, and conscience. To argue otherwise for abortion alone is special pleading.

**Furthermore, the data wielded against criminalization must be scrutinized at its source.** The statistics most frequently cited by advocates of decriminalization come from organizations such as the **Guttmacher Institute** — an organization founded as the research arm of Planned Parenthood, the world's largest abortion provider, and that continues to advocate explicitly for the worldwide expansion of abortion access. Much of the WHO's guidance on abortion has been developed in close partnership with Guttmacher and similar advocacy groups, and the WHO itself has in recent years openly taken an advocacy posture, publishing guidelines calling for the full decriminalization of abortion globally. This is not neutral science. This is activism wearing the robes of research.

Nigerians should be deeply suspicious of data produced by organizations whose institutional existence depends on the expansion of abortion. When Planned Parenthood's research arm tells us that restricting abortion doesn't reduce abortion, we should understand this as a sales pitch, not a finding. No honest observer would accept tobacco-company research claiming that cigarettes are harmless. We should extend no greater credulity to abortion-industry research claiming that abortion laws are ineffective.

## The "Safe Abortion" Argument Is a Distortion

Advocates of decriminalization often argue that criminal prohibition drives women to unsafe abortions and that legalization would save lives. This argument, though emotionally appealing, collapses under scrutiny. It is morally incoherent: it asks us to legalize the intentional killing of one human being in order to make that killing "safer" for the one doing it. By the same logic, we might legalize any dangerous crime to make it safer for the perpetrator.

The real solutions to maternal mortality in Nigeria lie elsewhere — in improving general healthcare access, expanding maternal care, strengthening education, supporting pregnant women in crisis, and combating the poverty that often drives desperate decisions. Legalizing abortion would not address any of these root causes; it would merely normalize the ending of innocent lives and treat the womb as a place of danger rather than nurture.

## Protecting Women Means Protecting Mothers and Children Together

A truly pro-woman society does not offer abortion as a solution to hardship; it offers support, dignity, and community. The assumption that women need abortion to flourish is itself a profoundly pessimistic view of womanhood — one that treats pregnancy as a disease and motherhood as an obstacle. As we have seen, in the nations that have embraced abortion most enthusiastically, it is women — especially unborn girls — who have died in the greatest numbers. The rhetoric of "autonomy" has provided cover for the greatest gendered massacre in human history.

Nigerian culture has always honored mothers, and our laws should reinforce, not undermine, this honor by ensuring that no woman feels that killing her child is her only option. What is needed is not decriminalization but compassionate enforcement paired with robust social support: crisis pregnancy centers, adoption systems, maternal healthcare, economic support for struggling mothers, and moral formation that honors both mother and child. Criminal law sets the moral boundary; social investment provides the path forward within it.

## Enforcement Is a Matter of Justice

Laws that are not enforced are laws in name only. The current reality in Nigeria — where illegal abortions occur in clandestine clinics across our cities — is a failure not of the law itself but of enforcement. Those who perform illegal abortions, particularly the profiteering practitioners who exploit vulnerable women, must face the full weight of the law. Justice demands no less. The unborn cannot speak for themselves, cannot march in protest, cannot vote. Precisely for that reason, the state has a heightened duty to speak for them through vigorous enforcement.

## Conclusion

Nigeria must not bend to international pressure, nor to the seductive but hollow arguments of those who would redefine human life for the sake of convenience. The unborn child is a human being, fully deserving of the protection of our laws — and simultaneously, a future citizen whose life is woven into the destiny of the nation. Our Constitution affirms the right to life; our Christian and Muslim faith traditions — embraced by over 95% of our people — affirm with one voice that life is a sacred gift from God; our culture affirms the sanctity of life; our national interest affirms that our children are our greatest wealth; and the global record of legalized abortion shouts a warning that only the willfully deaf can ignore: where abortion goes, girls die, the disabled are exterminated, and the poor are targeted.

The criminalization of abortion is not cruelty — it is justice. It is not backwardness — it is moral clarity and strategic wisdom in an age of confusion. Let Nigeria stand firm. Let us maintain our laws, enforce them courageously, and build a society where every child — born and unborn, male and female, able-bodied and disabled, Igbo and Yoruba and Hausa and every other proud people of our great nation — is welcomed, protected, and cherished. In defending the unborn, we defend the very principle that makes any society worth preserving: that every human life, without exception, is sacred. And in defending that principle, we also defend the future of Nigeria itself — a nation whose greatest century still lies ahead, carried forward on the shoulders of the very generations we now choose to protect.

# The Sacred Charge: Why Nigeria Must Uphold and Strengthen the Criminalization of Abortion

## Thesis

Abortion is the unjust killing of an innocent human being. Nigeria's laws rightly recognize this truth, and those laws must not merely be preserved — they must be enforced with renewed vigor and moral seriousness. Every argument offered in favor of legalizing abortion in Nigeria collapses under the weight of biology, conscience, faith, national interest, and the blood-soaked global record of what legal abortion has actually done to women, the disabled, and the poor. This essay will demonstrate, through a single unbroken chain of reasoning, why the criminalization of abortion enshrined in Sections 228, 229, and 230 of our Criminal Code and Sections 232–234 of our Penal Code reflects not backwardness but the highest wisdom — and why Nigerian legislators, religious leaders, and citizens must now act decisively to strengthen, not surrender, this moral foundation.

## I. The Foundation: The Unborn Child Is a Human Being, and Human Beings Have the Right to Life

Every argument in defense of the unborn begins — and must begin — with a single, undeniable fact: human life begins at conception. At the moment of fertilization, a new organism with distinct human DNA, separate from both mother and father, comes into being. This is not religion. It is not philosophy. It is embryology, taught in every medical school in the world. From that first moment, the unborn child is not a part of the mother's body but a distinct human being residing within it, growing according to its own genetic program, proceeding inexorably toward birth unless violently interrupted.

To deny personhood to this human being on the basis of size, location, level of development, or degree of dependency is to engage in precisely the kind of arbitrary line-drawing that history has always used to strip rights from the inconvenient. The same logic that permits the killing of the unborn because they are small, hidden, developing, and dependent also threatens the newborn, the disabled, the comatose, and the elderly. There is no coherent principle by which one may deny humanity to the child in the womb while affirming it for the child outside. Either every human being possesses inherent dignity — or none does.

Section 33(1) of the Constitution of the Federal Republic of Nigeria guarantees every person the right to life. That guarantee is either meaningful or it is meaningless. It cannot be stretched to cover some human beings while excluding others on grounds of convenience. The foundational argument is therefore simple and unanswerable: **the unborn child is a human being; human beings possess the right to life; therefore the unborn child possesses the right to life, and the deliberate killing of the unborn child is homicide.** Every subsequent argument in this essay builds upon this foundation.

## II. The Foundation Reinforced: The Unified Voice of Nigerian Faith

If the first argument establishes that the unborn child is a human being by the testimony of science and reason, the second establishes that the unborn child is a sacred being by the testimony of faith — a testimony that in Nigeria is not fragmented or contested but overwhelmingly unified. Over 95% of Nigerians identify as either Christian or Muslim, and on the question of abortion, these two great traditions speak with one voice. This is not a coincidence; it is a convergence of moral truth grasped through two distinct paths of revelation. And it means that legalizing abortion in Nigeria would not be a neutral reform — it would be an assault on the religious conscience of virtually the entire Nigerian people.

The Christian witness is absolute. The Catholic Bishops' Conference of Nigeria has, through repeated pastoral letters, condemned abortion as a grave moral evil and an offense against God, child, and mother alike. This teaching — rooted in two thousand years of unbroken Christian tradition — is echoed without hesitation across the Christian Association of Nigeria, the Anglican Communion, the Redeemed Christian Church of God, Deeper Life, the Pentecostal Fellowship, and every major Christian body in the nation. On this question, Nigerian Christianity is not divided.

The Islamic witness is equally firm. All four Sunni schools of jurisprudence — Maliki, Hanafi, Shafi'i, and Hanbali — condemn abortion; after ensoulment, every madhab regards it as categorically forbidden, tantamount to the killing of a human soul. A great many scholars, including prominent jurists within the Maliki tradition that shapes Northern Nigerian Islamic practice, prohibit abortion from the moment of conception. The Qur'an itself declares: *"Do not kill your children for fear of poverty — We provide for them and for you. Surely the killing of them is a great sin"* (17:31). The preservation of life (*hifz al-nafs*) is a foundational objective of the Shariah. To defend the unborn is, for the Nigerian Muslim, a religious duty.

Thus the science of embryology and the conscience of Nigerian faith converge on the same truth: the unborn child is a human being and must be protected. When Christians and Muslims — so often divided on other matters — stand together on this question, no Nigerian legislator possesses the moral authority to override that consensus. To do so would be to legislate against the soul of the nation in service of a small, foreign-funded elite. That is not democracy. **That is moral colonialism, and it must be refused.**

## III. The Stakes Widened: Nigeria's Destiny Depends on Her Children

The protection of the unborn is therefore a moral and religious imperative. But it is more. It is also a matter of national survival and national greatness. Having established that abortion is the killing of a human being and that Nigerian faith and conscience condemn it, we must now grasp the further truth that every unborn Nigerian is also a future citizen — a future laborer, innovator, teacher, defender, parent, and leader of the Nigerian nation.

Nigeria is projected to become the **third most populous country in the world by 2050**, surpassing the United States. With a median age under twenty, our nation possesses what economists call a "demographic dividend" — a youthful, dynamic population that is the single greatest strategic asset any nation can possess in the 21st century. Oil runs dry. Commodity prices collapse. Currencies devalue. But a growing, vibrant, faithful population is the one foundation on which enduring national greatness is built. Every nation that has risen to global power in modern history has done so on the backs of its children.

China — having aborted and coerced its way through decades of population control — now faces a demographic collapse so severe that its government frantically begs its citizens to have children. Japan, South Korea, and most of Europe confront the same grim arithmetic: aging societies, shrinking workforces, collapsing pension systems, empty villages, and desperate reliance on mass immigration to replace the children they chose not to bear. These nations would give almost anything to possess what Nigeria possesses today.

To legalize abortion in Nigeria would be to voluntarily inflict upon ourselves the wound that is killing other civilizations. It would mean the extinguishment of millions of Nigerian lives before their first breath — farmers who would feed our continent, scientists who would solve our problems, soldiers who would guard our borders, thinkers who would articulate a Nigerian vision for the world. Each aborted child is not merely a life destroyed; it is a contribution to the nation forever lost. **A Nigeria of four hundred million confident, educated, faithful citizens will be a superpower. A Nigeria that has aborted a generation of her children will be a warning to the world.**

## IV. The Horror Exposed: Legal Abortion Is a Weapon Against the Weak

We have established that abortion kills a human being, that Nigerian faith forbids it, and that Nigeria's future demands the protection of her unborn. We now arrive at the most devastating argument of all — the argument that destroys every remaining illusion about what legal abortion actually does in the world. Wherever abortion has been legalized, it has become a weapon — wielded with particular savagery against girls, against the disabled, and against ethnic minorities. How long before prenatal screening becomes a tool for purging the "unfit" from our society?

**The war on minorities has been the defining reality of abortion in the West.** In the United States, Black Americans — roughly 13% of the population — account for over a third of all abortions. Black women abort at nearly **four times the rate** of white women. In New York City, more Black babies are aborted than are born alive. This is not an accident, nor is it a social tragedy that abortion providers regret. **Margaret Sanger**, the founder of Planned Parenthood — the organization whose research arm (the Guttmacher Institute) manufactures most of the statistics used by the global pro-abortion lobby — was an unapologetic eugenicist. She wrote of limiting the reproduction of those she called "unfit," engaged white supremacist networks to spread birth control in Black communities through her so-called "Negro Project," and spoke openly of reducing the populations of "inferior" groups. The organization she founded has fulfilled her vision with terrible precision.

**This is the future that pro-abortion advocates would import into Nigeria.** We are a nation of extraordinary ethnic diversity — Igbo, Yoruba, Hausa-Fulani, Ijaw, Tiv, Kanuri, Efik, Ibibio, and hundreds of other proud peoples. Cultural preferences for sons persist in many communities. Dowry customs, inheritance patterns, and patrilineal expectations create precisely the social conditions in which sex-selective abortion has flourished elsewhere. Stigma around disability remains real. Poorer communities would — as they have everywhere — become the prime targets of abortion providers setting up clinics under the banner of "reproductive health." How many Nigerian daughters would never be born? How many Nigerian children with disabilities would be eliminated? How many of our poorest communities would be quietly decimated?

Thus the final, shattering truth: **legal abortion is not liberation. It is the most effective instrument ever devised for killing girls, eliminating the disabled, and suppressing ethnic minorities — and it is women and the marginalized who have paid its bloodiest price.** Any Nigerian who invokes "women's autonomy" in support of abortion has either not examined the global record or chosen to ignore it. To protect the unborn is to protect women — not in spite of them, but for them. To criminalize abortion is to stand with the weakest against the cruelest application of power ever dressed in the language of freedom.

## V. The Myths Demolished: "Safety" and "Ineffectiveness" Are Lies

Having established the horror that legal abortion has unleashed upon the world's most vulnerable, we may now dispose of the two tired talking points that pro-abortion advocates still recycle in Nigerian public debate.

The first is the claim that criminalization produces "unsafe abortion" and costs lives. This argument is morally incoherent. It demands that we legalize the deliberate killing of one human being in order to make that killing safer for the killer. By such logic, we should legalize every dangerous crime to reduce risk to its perpetrators. The real drivers of maternal mortality in Nigeria — inadequate healthcare infrastructure, poverty, lack of skilled birth attendants, weak emergency obstetric care — have nothing to do with abortion law and everything to do with the broader condition of our health system. These are the problems that demand investment. Legalizing the killing of children does not build hospitals; it merely redirects the killing from back-alley clinics to licensed ones, while multiplying it many times over, as the experience of every liberalizing country confirms.

The second is the claim that "criminalization doesn't reduce abortion." This is simply false. **Malta**, with among the strictest abortion laws in Europe, records among the lowest abortion rates in the developed world. **Poland**, which tightened its laws further in recent years, maintains rates vastly lower than its permissive neighbors. **Ireland**, before its 2018 referendum, had one of the lowest abortion rates in the West precisely because of its legal and cultural defense of the unborn. Meanwhile Britain, France, Sweden, and the United States — all permissive — record rates many times higher. Laws shape behavior. Laws shape culture. Laws shape conscience. We do not argue that murder statutes fail to reduce murder or that theft statutes fail to reduce theft. The claim that abortion law alone is uniquely impotent is special pleading of the crudest kind.

And whence comes the "evidence" for this claim? Overwhelmingly from the **Guttmacher Institute** — founded as the research arm of Planned Parenthood and still an open advocacy organization for the global expansion of abortion — and from WHO documents increasingly authored in partnership with such advocacy groups. This is not science. This is the tobacco industry producing research on cigarettes; the arms dealer producing research on peace. Nigerian legislators, journalists, and citizens must treat it with the skepticism it deserves.

## VI. The Call to Action

The argument is now complete. It rises in a single unbroken chain: the unborn child is a human being (I); the conscience of Nigeria's Christians and Muslims unanimously affirms this (II); the destiny of the Nigerian nation depends upon the protection of her coming generations (III); legalized abortion has functioned globally as a weapon against women, the disabled, and the poor (IV); and every remaining argument for legalization is a demonstrable falsehood, much of it manufactured by the very industry that profits from abortion (V). No counter-argument remains standing. The conclusion is inescapable: **Nigeria must uphold, enforce, and strengthen her laws against abortion.**

Therefore:

**To the legislators of the Federal Republic of Nigeria** — in the National Assembly and in every State House of Assembly — we issue this demand: preserve without amendment Sections 228, 229, and 230 of the Criminal Code and Sections 232, 233, and 234 of the Penal Code. Reject every proposal, foreign or domestic, that seeks to weaken, broaden exceptions to, or decriminalize any aspect of these provisions. Go further: pass legislation that strengthens penalties against those who perform illegal abortions, increases funding for maternal healthcare and crisis pregnancy support, criminalizes the importation and distribution of abortifacient drugs, and closes the loopholes through which clandestine abortion networks operate in our cities. You were elected by a people whose faith and conscience demand the protection of life. Honor that mandate.

**To the religious leaders of Nigeria** — bishops of the Catholic Church, leaders of CAN, imams and ulama of the Sultanate and of every emirate and mosque across the federation — we call upon you to speak with one voice and without hesitation. Preach the sanctity of life from every pulpit and every minbar. Organize jointly across confessional lines in defense of the unborn, demonstrating to Nigeria and the world the unity of her faithful. Hold politicians accountable. Equip the young with the moral clarity they will need to resist the seductions of a dying Western ideology. The unborn have no voice but yours.

**To the citizens of Nigeria** — of every ethnicity, every region, every station — we say: do not be deceived. The forces pressing for abortion in our country are not neutral reformers. They are heirs of a movement built on eugenics, sustained by profit, and proven in practice to kill girls, eliminate the disabled, and target the poor. Defend your laws. Defend your faith. Defend your nation. Support the mothers in your families and your communities who face difficult pregnancies, so that no Nigerian woman is ever made to feel that killing her child is her only option. Raise sons who honor women and daughters who know their worth. Vote, speak, write, and pray in defense of the unborn.

Nigeria stands at a threshold. Behind us lies the wisdom of our ancestors, the testimony of our faiths, and the laws that have defended our children. Before us lies a choice: to preserve that inheritance and rise into the great nation we are destined to become, or to surrender it and join the dying civilizations that have aborted their own futures. Let our answer be unambiguous. Let it be spoken in every church and every mosque, in every legislative chamber and every village square. **Nigeria chooses life. Nigeria defends her children. Nigeria will not abort her future.**

The unborn are watching. History is watching. God is watching. Let us be found worthy.
